pages.title: ADB Hydropower Growth Nepal Study
pages.excerpt: ADB assessment of Nepal's hydropower development trajectory, covering growth patterns, project pipeline, and development constraints. Extracted by Hermes in Wave 2.
pages.body_text: ADB Hydropower Growth Nepal Study # Summary ADB assessment of Nepal's hydropower development trajectory, covering growth patterns, project pipeline, and development constraints. Extracted by Hermes in Wave 2. # Key Data Points Documents the IPP-driven expansion model Identifies financing, terrain, and institutional bottlenecks Provides context for buildability constraints # Relevance to Project Background source for Hermes Wave 2 engineering and buildability analysis. Extracted text stored in . Provides context for understanding why nepal-energy-profile grew through IPPs rather than state-led mega-projects.

pages.title: Arun-3 Project Status & PDA Summary (2025)
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 900 MW Arun-3 Hydroelectric Project — combining the 2014 Project Development Agreement (PDA) between Nepal and SJVN with 2024–2025 construction progress reports.
pages.body_text: Arun-3 Project Status & PDA Summary (2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 900 MW Arun-3 Hydroelectric Project — combining the 2014 Project Development Agreement (PDA) between Nepal and SJVN with 2024–2025 construction progress reports. # Key Data Points Project basics Installed capacity: 900 MW (4 × 225 MW Francis turbines) Location: Phaksinda (dam) to Pukhuwa (powerhouse), Sankhuwasabha District Design: peaking run-of-river, 3.4 hours/day at full rated output Estimated cost: USD 1.6+ billion Annual energy: 3,924–4,019 GWh (design) Concession: 30-year Build-Own-Operate-Transfer (BOOT) Commercial structure (PDA, 2014) Developer: SJVN (Satluj Jal Vidyut Nigam), via subsidiary SAPDC Nepal's free-power share: 21.9% (~197 MW equivalent, royalty-free) Additional 10% offered to Nepal on commercial terms Remaining electricity exported to India via a new 400 kV DC line to Sitamarhi, Bihar Schedule and progress (December 2025) Construction start: 2018/19 Original COD target: March 2025 — not met SJVN applied for COD extension to September 2026; Project Review Panel formed to decide Physical progress: 74.61% (power generation side) Financial progress: 74.6% 400 kV cross-border transmission line: 58.3% physical progress # Relevance to Project Primary source for Arun-3's capacity, schedule, cost, and the Nepal–India free-power / export structure that makes Arun-3 a flagship example of India-developed, India-dispatched hydropower inside Nepal. # What This Source Does Not Cover Independent cost audit (SJVN figures are self-reported) Social / resettlement impact assessment outside SAPDC's own materials Seasonal generation shape beyond the 3.4-hour peaking design number Grid-side absorption constraints on the Indian side # See also arun-3 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation

pages.title: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record
headings: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project, covering the original 1980s ADB-supported feasibility, the 2021 restart with land acquisition, the 2024 NEA construction proposal to government, and the 2024–2025 financing conversations with AIIB.
pages.body_text: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record # Summary Reference compilation for the Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project, covering the original 1980s ADB-supported feasibility, the 2021 restart with land acquisition, the 2024 NEA construction proposal to government, and the 2024–2025 financing conversations with AIIB. # Key Data Points Capacity: 635 MW (NEA / Investopaper) or 670 MW (ELC / Power Technology) — design basis varies between the two references Main powerhouse: 600 MW underground + 70 MW dam-toe ecological flow unit Annual energy (design): 3,377–3,444 GWh/year Dam: 200–220 m height at Lamidanda / Rabhuwaghat Headrace tunnel: 13.3 km, reservoir to Dhitung powerhouse Active reservoir volume: 1.581 MCM (per Power Technology) Estimated total cost: ~USD 2.2 billion (~NPR 308 billion) Districts: Okhaldhunga, Khotang, Solukhumbu Developer: Nepal Electricity Authority Financing track: AIIB sovereign funding proposed Schedule: construction from ~2027, commercial operation ~2031 (ELC / Power Technology estimate) Land: ~20,000 ropani private, ~162 households fully displaced, ~988 partially affected # Relevance to Project Primary source for Dudhkoshi's technical envelope, financing posture, social footprint and timeline — the three axes on which its buildability will actually be judged. Note: two self-consistent but divergent capacity numbers (635 MW vs 670 MW) are present in respected secondary sources; this page records both. # What This Source Does Not Cover Detailed seismic design basis (the project sits adjacent to Everest- region fault systems) Reservoir-induced seismicity modelling GLOF integration into the dam's design flood (the 2024 Thame GLOF in the same river system is directly relevant) # See also dudhkoshi-storage glof-risk storage-deficit

pages.title: Green Hydrogen Roadmap for Nepal
pages.excerpt: Strategic roadmap for producing green hydrogen from Nepal's surplus monsoon hydropower via electrolysis. Envisions an entirely new domestic industrial ecosystem absorbing stranded wet-season power.
pages.body_text: Green Hydrogen Roadmap for Nepal # Summary Strategic roadmap for producing green hydrogen from Nepal's surplus monsoon hydropower via electrolysis. Envisions an entirely new domestic industrial ecosystem absorbing stranded wet-season power. # Key Data Points Primary target: domestic green ammonia/fertilizer production (Nepal imports 100% of chemical fertilizers) A domestic green fertilizer plant could absorb up to 500 MW of continuous baseload power Replacing coal in Nepal's cement clinker production would require 227,743 tonnes of green hydrogen annually, needing 2,002 MW of dedicated hydropower Projected viable production cost: $2.15/kg by 2035 Government adopted Green Hydrogen Policy in 2024 with tax exemptions and regulatory framework Commercial pilots targeted for 2025–2027, industrial scale by 2035 # Relevance to Project Central to green-hydrogen-nepal and the demand-creation argument in domestic-led-hydro-strategy. Addresses stranded-generation by creating domestic demand for surplus monsoon power. Only Gemini covered this in depth. Gemini ref 10.

pages.title: ICIMOD HKH Glacier Change Summary
pages.excerpt: Regional glacier change assessment for the Hindu Kush Himalaya. Documents the scale and pace of glacier retreat affecting Nepal's river systems and long-term hydropower viability.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD HKH Glacier Change Summary # Summary Regional glacier change assessment for the Hindu Kush Himalaya. Documents the scale and pace of glacier retreat affecting Nepal's river systems and long-term hydropower viability. # Key Data Points Glacier area across HKH fell by ~12% between 1990 and 2020, with faster recent ice loss Nepal-specific inventories show substantial long-run glacier retreat Glacier retreat rate: ~0.4–0.5m water equivalent per year Under high-emission scenarios, 65% glacier volume reduction projected by 2100 # Relevance to Project Underpins peak-water concept and glof-risk. Relevant to koshi-basin (278 glaciers, 51 km³ ice). The 12% area loss and 65% volume projection are key numbers for the climate dimension of the video essay. Codex [S13], also used by Claude for the peak-water framing (2040–2070 build window).

pages.title: ICIMOD Koshi Basin Programme
pages.excerpt: ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD Koshi Basin Programme # Summary ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system. # Key Data Points Koshi basin supports >40 million people Contributes disproportionately high sediment loads to the Ganges system Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar The Koshi is known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to shifting course and catastrophic flooding # Relevance to Project Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution. Also relevant to sediment-as-design-constraint given extreme sediment loads. Codex [S9].

pages.title: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin
headings: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin
pages.excerpt: Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin # Summary Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint. # Key Data Points Koshi carries extreme sediment loads into the Ganges system Sediment, flood, and erosion risks are interconnected Climate change is intensifying GLOF, landslide, and sediment-pulse risks # Relevance to Project Supports sediment-as-design-constraint, glof-risk, and the hazard dimension of buildability. Codex [S10].

pages.title: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018)
headings: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018)
pages.excerpt: India's regulatory framework governing cross-border electricity trade. Defines approval mechanisms, designated authorities, and conditions for Nepal's electricity exports to India. Critically, includes restrictions on projects with certain foreign investment.
pages.body_text: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018) # Summary India's regulatory framework governing cross-border electricity trade. Defines approval mechanisms, designated authorities, and conditions for Nepal's electricity exports to India. Critically, includes restrictions on projects with certain foreign investment. # Key Data Points Establishes Indian approval requirements for Nepal power imports India explicitly refuses purchase of electricity from Nepali projects with Chinese equity investment or Chinese EPC contractors Creates the monopsony market structure Nepal must navigate for exports Shapes which projects can access the Indian market vs which are locked out # Relevance to Project Central to india-energy-relationship and hydro-geopolitics. Shapes the export constraint dimension of seasonal-arbitrage-trap. Hermes Wave 3 source. Gemini highlighted the China-exclusion mechanism. [!gap] Need updated post-2024 versions of these guidelines — the approval regime may have expanded.

pages.title: JICA IPSDP Final Report Annexes (Vol. 2)
headings: What this source does not cover
pages.excerpt: This is the Annexes volume (Vol. 2) of the JICA Study Team's Final Report for the (IPSDP) — the same study whose compact policy conclusions are captured in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025. The executive summary is where the headline numbers and milestone ladder live; this volume i
pages.body_text: JICA IPSDP Final Report Annexes (Vol. 2) # Summary This is the Annexes volume (Vol. 2) of the JICA Study Team's Final Report for the (IPSDP) — the same study whose compact policy conclusions are captured in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025. The executive summary is where the headline numbers and milestone ladder live; this volume is where the _workings_ live — the demand forecast inputs, the MP2014 project scorecards, the existing-plant inventories, the hydropower sequencing logic, and the geology, hydrology and seismology that bound what can actually be built. The volume is 315 pages and contains Annexes 1–6 only. The PDF's own table of contents lists 16 annexes in total; Annexes 7–16 (power generation planning, cross-border trade status, power system planning, SEA, economic and financial analysis, financing, surplus utilisation, governance, milestones, capacity development) are carried in companion volumes that are not currently in the wiki's raw-source set. For this wiki, this is the authoritative upstream source for any claim of the form "JICA/MoEWRI's IPSDP study assumes X" about demand, existing fleet state, hydropower portfolio composition, or development-plan review — the executive summary is not granular enough on its own. Key cross-links: moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, nea, seasonal-arbitrage-trap, kulekhani-cascade, kali-gandaki-a, tanahu-hydropower, upper-arun. # Contents (Annexes 1–6 only) Annex | Title | First page | -------|-------|-----------:| 1 | General Information of Nepal (economy, topography, geology, seismology, climate, hydrology, air quality) | 5 | 2 | Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal (organisational structure, legal system, reform, corporate entities) | 55 | 3 | Current Situation of the Electric Power Facilities (existing generation fleet, transmission, distribution) | 146 | 4 | Review of Existing Development Plans (MP2014 screening, Multi-Criteria Analysis scorecards) | 174 | 5 | Power Demand Forecast (sectoral sales mix, consumer growth, electrification) | 196 | 6 | Hydropower Development Plan (bulk of the volume; portfolio composition and sequencing) | 244 | Annexes 7–16 listed in the front-matter TOC (Power Generation Development Planning; Current Status of Power Trade; Power System Development Planning; SEA; Economic and Financial Analysis; Financing; Utilisation of Surplus Electricity; Governance; Milestones & Future Outlook; Capacity Development) are _not present_ in this PDF. # Key claims Annex 3 (p. 147) states that NEA owns hydropower plants with an installed capacity of and designed annual generation of at an average plant factor of . Only Kulekhani I and Kulekhani II operate as storage (STO); the rest are ROR or PROR. Annex 3 generation-type table (p. 146) codifies four hydropower types used throughout the IPSDP optimisation — , (peak run-of-river with daily-to-weekly pondage), (storage / reservoir), and (pumped storage) — and notes the winter-peak / monsoon-spill economics that drive the seasonal-arbitrage-trap argument. Annex 4 (p. 174–175) records how the 2014 Nationwide Master Plan (MP2014) filtered candidate projects via Multi-Criteria Analysis with exclusions for installed capacity , dam height , project cost , annual flow-regulation rate , reservoir resettlement , or sites inside national parks / wildlife conservation areas / world heritage. The MP2014 scorecard table on p. 175 lists unit capacity, total energy, dry energy, project cost, unit cost, EIRR and FIRR for Dudh Koshi, Sun Koshi 3, Lower Badigad, Andhi Khola, Chera-1, Madi, Nalsyau Gad, Naumure (W. Rapti), Kokhajor-1, Lower Jhimruk and the rest of the MP2014 list. Annex 5 (p. 196) puts sectoral sales composition and consumer-growth rate at approximately with household electrification reaching in 2021 and a stated GoN target of . # Local source Local workspace copy: jica_ipsdp_main_report_vol2.pdf [!cite] jica-ipsdp-main-report-vol2 PDF created via Word → Adobe PDF. File is byte-identical to in the same directory (verified via SHA-256); the canonical path used by the wiki is . # What this source does not cover Annexes 7–16 of the IPSDP final report (power system planning, cross-border trade, SEA, financing, surplus-electricity utilisation, governance, milestone ladder, capacity development). Use moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 for the compact statement of those or queue companion JICA volumes when available. Monthly plant-level generation or trade settlement data. Project-by-project post-study construction progress after the 2024 cutoff.

pages.title: Kali Gandaki A — ADB Performance Evaluation & Wikipedia
headings: Kali Gandaki A — ADB Performance Evaluation & Wikipedia
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 144 MW Kali Gandaki A plant at Mirmi, Syangja — combining the ADB's post-project performance evaluation (the plant was ADB/JBIC co-financed) with the Wikipedia-aggregated operational history and the recent NEA rehabilitation tender.
pages.body_text: Kali Gandaki A — ADB Performance Evaluation & Wikipedia # Summary Reference compilation for the 144 MW Kali Gandaki A plant at Mirmi, Syangja — combining the ADB's post-project performance evaluation (the plant was ADB/JBIC co-financed) with the Wikipedia-aggregated operational history and the recent NEA rehabilitation tender. # Key Data Points Installed: 144 MW (3 × 48 MW Toshiba Francis units) Gross head: 115 m Design annual energy: ~860 GWh Average actual output: ~592 GWh/year since commissioning Construction: 1997–2002; turbines commissioned Mar / Apr / May 2002 Total cost: USD 354.8 million (~NPR 50 billion at the time) Funding: ADB + Japan Bank for International Cooperation Owner/operator: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA) Location: Mirmi, Syangja District; Kali Gandaki–Andhikhola confluence Status (2024): Nepal sought design & supervision consultants for a rehabilitation of the three-unit plant # Relevance to Project Primary source for Kali Gandaki A's capacity, head, generation, funding stack and rehabilitation posture. Useful benchmark for Nepali- developed, Nepali-dispatched hydro at moderate scale — the foil to India-developed export projects like arun-3 and upper-karnali. # What This Source Does Not Cover Current reservoir (pondage) operation strategy Full seasonal generation shape Detailed explanation of the >250 GWh gap between design energy (~860 GWh) and average actual output (~592 GWh) # See also kali-gandaki-a gandaki-basin run-of-river-hydropower

pages.title: MoEWRI IPSDP Executive Summary
headings: What this source does not cover
pages.excerpt: This executive summary is the clearest compact statement of the current MoEWRI/JICA planning logic for Nepal's long-horizon power buildout through 2040. It ties together demand growth, hydropower sequencing, 400 kV backbone and interconnection expansion, export strategy, and fina
pages.body_text: MoEWRI IPSDP Executive Summary # Summary This executive summary is the clearest compact statement of the current MoEWRI/JICA planning logic for Nepal's long-horizon power buildout through 2040. It ties together demand growth, hydropower sequencing, 400 kV backbone and interconnection expansion, export strategy, and financing needs in one document instead of scattering them across older nea or donor studies. The report is especially valuable because page 39 turns the plan into a milestone ladder with explicit dates for export crossover, self-sufficiency, the 28 GW government target, and a later clean-export role in South Asia. Section 2.4 then links those milestones to concrete trade channels, grid buildout, and wholesale arrangements with India and Bangladesh rather than treating exports as an abstract objective. Section 3 adds the guardrails the plan says must shape buildability, including environmental-flow management, protected-area restrictions, and the need to package hydropower with transmission and institutional reform. For this wiki, the document is a backbone source for india-energy-relationship, bangladesh-trade-route, niettp, and project-sequencing questions that depend on an official state planning frame rather than a single project promoter's view. # Contents Section | Page | ---------|------| 1. Introduction | 1 | 1.1 Background | 1 | 1.2 Objectives | 1 | 1.3 Basic Approaches on IPSDP | 2 | 2. IPSDP | 3 | 2.1 Outline of IPSDP | 3 | 2.2 Basic Understandings of the Development in Power Sector | 3 | 2.2.1 Organizational Structure of the Power Sector | 3 | 2.2.2 Involvement of Existing and On-going Power Development Plans in the Power Sector | 4 | 2.2.3 Hydropower Development Plan | 5 | 2.2.4 Power Trade | 7 | 2.3 The Study for Development Scenarios | 8 | 2.3.1 Direction of Development Scenarios | 8 | 2.3.2 Setting of Development Scenarios | 10 | 2.3.3 Analysis Results of Development Scenarios and Selection of Optimum Scenario | 12 | 2.4 Formulation of Optimum Scenario | 12 | 2.4.1 Power Demand Forecast | 14 | 2.4.2 Power Generation Development Planning | 15 | 2.4.3 The Power System Plan (PSP) and power trade with neighboring countries | 18 | 2.4.4 Financial Analysis of IPSDP | 22 | 2.4.5 Investment Plan | 28 | 2.4.6 Strategic Environmental Assessment | 31 | 3. Development Milestones and Future Outlook for IPSDP | 34 | 3.1 Development Milestones and Pathways towards 2040 in IPSDP | 34 | 3.2 Challenges of IPSDP | 36 | 3.3 Required Transformations and Future Outlook for the Power Sector | 37 | 3.4 Challenges and Recommendations for IPSDP | 39 | # Key claims Figure 3.1-1 on page 39 marks as the year when power exports surpass imports. The same milestone ladder marks as the point when self-sufficiency in electricity supply is achieved. The ladder marks as the year when the government's 28,000 MW development target is achieved. The ladder marks as the stage when Nepal contributes clean export to the South Asian region. The page-39 era buckets attach named projects to each window: Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen, Upper Modi A, Rahughat, Upper Trishuli 3B, Madhya Bhotekoshi, Upper Trishuli-1, and Arun 3 in the block; Tanahu HEP, Tamor Storage, Madi Storage HEP, Manang Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki Ka, Bheri 4, Tila-1, and Tila-2 in ; Lower Seti, Uttarganga STO, Phukot Karnali, Nalsyau Gad STO, Upper Arun, Chainpur Seti, Dudhkoshi STO, Sunkoshi 3 STO, Lower Badigad STO, Budhi Gandaki, Upper Marsyangdi-2, Dudh Koshi-IV, Upper Tamor, Bheri-2, West Seti STO, and SR-06 STO in ; and Betan Karnali, Kimathanka Arun, Bharbung, Mugu Karnali STO, Tamakoshi 3, Khimti Shivalaya STO, Bajhang Upper Seti, Naumure STO, Upper Mugu Karnali, Bheri-1, and the Humla Karnali Cascade in . Section 2.4 frames the wholesale-trade menu around NEA offtake, power-trading companies, and direct overseas trade to DISCOs/customers in India and Bangladesh, with the adjacent text on pages 24-26 adding grid-connection, market, and long-term cross-border PPA mechanics that sit behind those channels. The SEA mitigation table on page 37 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows with reference to the IFC 2018 handbook, and it says projects in protected areas defined by Nepal's National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act require prior permission and cannot assume river modification is automatically permissible. [!cite] moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 Cover date is December 2024 even though the local filename ends in 2025; the milestone ladder and policy framing cited above come from pages 25, 37, and 39 of the PDF. # Local source Local workspace copy: moewri_ipsdp_exec_summary_2025.pdf # What this source does not cover project-by-project construction progress after the study cutoff plant-level monthly generation or trade data # See also data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 nepal-power-trade-schemes environmental-flow-policy ipsdp-2025-vs-wiki-claims

pages.title: Mugu Karnali Storage — Feasibility, Bids & EOI Record (2024–2025)
headings: Mugu Karnali Storage — Feasibility, Bids & EOI Record (2024–2025)
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 1,902 MW Mugu Karnali Storage Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest proposed generation asset, in pre-feasibility under Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUC) and open to public–private partnership bidding.
pages.body_text: Mugu Karnali Storage — Feasibility, Bids & EOI Record (2024–2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 1,902 MW Mugu Karnali Storage Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest proposed generation asset, in pre-feasibility under Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUC) and open to public–private partnership bidding. # Key Data Points Capacity: 1,902 MW Type: Storage / pumped-storage; 10 Francis turbines Gross head: ~285 m Design annual energy: ~5,883 GWh River: Karnali District: Mugu (registry); Bajura also referenced in early materials Developer: Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUCL / VUC) — state-owned Structure: PPP, VUC seeking 51% partnership stake Survey licence: granted by DoED; 2-year feasibility deadline with extension possible to 5 years Expressions of interest (2024–2025): - Adani Group — EOI (no formal bid as of January 2025) - Chowdhury Group (CG Energo Pvt Ltd) — formal bid submitted, only competitor on record as of January 2025 Planned schedule (Power Technology): construction from ~2030, COD ~2034 # Relevance to Project Primary source for Mugu Karnali's scale, schedule, bidding posture and the India-vs-Bangladesh developer interest that makes it politically visible. Notable because 1,902 MW is larger than Nepal's entire current operating hydropower fleet. # What This Source Does Not Cover Detailed hydrology / sediment / seismic study outcomes Transmission evacuation plan — a project this size implies new 400 kV or higher corridor infrastructure that does not yet exist Environmental / social scoping; project is at pre-feasibility and no full EIA has been disclosed # See also mugu-karnali-storage-hep karnali-basin storage-deficit

pages.title: National Water Plan, Nepal
pages.excerpt: Older but widely cited national planning document for Nepal's water resources. Provides the foundational estimates of Nepal's contribution to the Ganges system and total annual runoff.
pages.body_text: National Water Plan, Nepal # Summary Older but widely cited national planning document for Nepal's water resources. Provides the foundational estimates of Nepal's contribution to the Ganges system and total annual runoff. # Key Data Points Nepal's total average annual runoff: ~225 BCM Nepal-origin rivers contribute ~40% of Ganges mean annual flow Nepal-origin rivers contribute ~70% of Ganges dry-season flow at Farakka Per-unit-area freshwater output among the highest on earth # Relevance to Project Source for the ganges-contribution numbers used across all providers. Codex [S1]. The 40%/70% figures are central to hydro-geopolitics and india-energy-relationship. [!warning] These figures are historical estimates. The exact percentages should be treated cautiously — the underlying hydrology predates recent climate shifts — but the strategic direction remains intact.

pages.title: NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: The most current official snapshot of Nepal's electricity system. Covers installed capacity, generation mix, trade volumes, grid infrastructure, and project pipeline for fiscal year 2024/25 (Nepali FY 2081/82).
pages.body_text: NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 # Summary The most current official snapshot of Nepal's electricity system. Covers installed capacity, generation mix, trade volumes, grid infrastructure, and project pipeline for fiscal year 2024/25 (Nepali FY 2081/82). # Image Catalog This run is ingesting roughly 130 images from the FY 2024/25 annual report; the current atlas pass surfaced 131 real-image candidates for review. The atlas manifest lives at nea_annual_report_2024_2025_image_atlas.json. # Key Data Points Total installed generation capacity: 3,591.262 MW (hydro 3,389.912 MW, solar 141.940 MW, thermal 53.410 MW, bagasse 6 MW) Hydropower is 94.4% of installed generation 22 new projects (434 MW combined) commissioned in FY 2024/25 Available energy: 15,641 GWh (34% NEA/subsidiaries, 55% IPPs, 11% imported from India) kulekhani-cascade generated 279.8 GWh tanahu-hydropower approximately 67% complete 572 projects in broader license pipeline (311 survey, 180 generation, 81 operation) # Relevance to Project Primary source for current installed capacity, fleet composition, generation split, and trade volumes. Used heavily by Codex [S4] and Gemini [ref 20]. Supersedes older NEA FY 2022/23 data used by Hermes. # What This Source Does Not Cover Seasonal monthly breakdown of generation and trade Plant-level curtailment data Audited financial statements (separate document) [!note] The World Bank's 2025 Economic Memo cites ~2,990 MW by end-2024. The difference from 3,591 MW reflects the date gap — NEA's report covers through mid-2025. Both are correct at their respective cutoff dates.

pages.title: NEA Transmission Annual Book 2077
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Official Nepal Electricity Authority annual-book publication for FY 2019/20 (B.S. 2077). The volume mixes directorate narrative, project status notes, site/substation photographs, appendix tables, and map pages. For the wiki, its highest-value material is in the transmission chap
pages.body_text: NEA Transmission Annual Book 2077 # Summary Official Nepal Electricity Authority annual-book publication for FY 2019/20 (B.S. 2077). The volume mixes directorate narrative, project status notes, site/substation photographs, appendix tables, and map pages. For the wiki, its highest-value material is in the transmission chapter and appendix: project-by-project line/substation status, existing substation capacity, and under-construction high-voltage grid inventory. # Contents Section | Page | ---------|------| Message from the Minister | 2 | Message from the Secretary | 4 | Board of Directorates | 5 | Organisation Structure | 6 | Deputy Managing Directors | 7 | Managing Director's Report | 8 | Generation Directorate | 21 | Transmission Directorate | 43 | Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate | 76 | Planning, Monitoring and Information Technology Directorate | 103 | Engineering Services Directorate | 110 | Project Management Directorate | 141 | NEA's Subsidiary & Associate Companies | 159 | Central Activities | 192 | Administrative Directorate | 199 | Finance Directorate | 204 | Statistic & Schematics | 220 | Electricity Tariff | 232-238 | Appendix | 239-263 | Map | 264-265 | # Relevance to Project This is the primary official source for NEA's mid-2020 view of the transmission system. It is especially useful for: hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone stage/status details dhalkebar-muzaffarpur substation and energization context appendix-level substation capacity and under-construction inventory pages project/program pages that need official inline figures rather than generic district scenery # Local Source Local workspace copy: nea_transmission_annual_book_2077.pdf # What This Source Does Not Cover post-2020 commissioning outcomes for projects still under construction here later FY 2024/25 system totals already superseded in nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 detailed load-flow or dispatch modelling

pages.title: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025)
headings: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025)
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for Nepal's current and planned high-voltage transmission assets — the 400 kV east–west backbone and parallel central corridor, the legacy 220 kV and 132 kV grid, and the cross-border interconnections with India (ten links at five voltage levels). Drawn from
pages.body_text: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025) # Summary Reference compilation for Nepal's current and planned high-voltage transmission assets — the 400 kV east–west backbone and parallel central corridor, the legacy 220 kV and 132 kV grid, and the cross-border interconnections with India (ten links at five voltage levels). Drawn from NEA's 2024/25 annual report, NEA Transmission Directorate publications, the Millennium Challenge Account Nepal (MCA-Nepal) compact programme documents, and news reporting of individual commissioning events. # Key Data Points 400 kV operational backbone Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa (HDDI) — first and currently only 400 kV east–west backbone inside Nepal. Commissioned in segments 2018–2022. Total spine ~300 km. Feeds the Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur cross-border link (400 kV bipole, operational since 2016, ADB + KfW co-financed). This is the sole operational 400 kV export route. 400 kV under construction / implementation MCA central 400 kV (Lapsiphedi–Ratmate–Hetauda–Damauli–Butwal) — the MCA-Nepal compact's headline 315 km transmission line, partially energised in 2025. Completion target 2026–2027. Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV — second cross-border link, under construction. Commissioning target mid-2026. Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV — third cross-border, implementation phase. Critical to Arun-3 evacuation once the plant is online. Lamki–Bareilly 400 kV — western cross-border at implementation setup; completion horizon 2027+. 220 kV operational Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV — original IPP evacuation corridor Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV — east–west spine at 220 kV Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV — west-central hydropower corridor 132 kV cross-border (legacy, operational) Kataiya–Kushaha (since 1971, the historic first link) Raxaul–Parwanipur, New Nautanwa–Mainahiya, Tanakpur–Mahendranagar Planned / under construction cross-border Nepalgunj–Nanpara 132 kV (under construction) Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV (planned, far-west) System-level parameters Nepal–India transfer capacity, 2025: ~2,300 MW total across all interconnections combined Current concurrent peak export: ~1,200 MW (monsoon 2024–25) Grid code: NEA grid code 2019 (amended 2022); Indian-side dispatch via NRLDC # Relevance to Project Primary source for the voltage levels, statuses, schedules and strategic roles of Nepal's ten currently-tracked corridors and ten cross-border interconnections. Used as the baseline citation for all per-corridor entity pages. # What This Source Does Not Cover Actual segment-level substation capacities (published only in confidential planning documents) Indian-side evacuation constraints beyond the border crossings Load flow studies under different generation dispatch scenarios Battery and STATCOM augmentation plans # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone dhalkebar-muzaffarpur mca-central-400 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation

pages.title: Tanahu Hydropower Project Status (ADB, 2024–2025)
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropower Project's construction progress and social-compliance posture, drawn from ADB's own project monitoring reports and two Accountability Mechanism complaints (2024 and 2025).
pages.body_text: Tanahu Hydropower Project Status (ADB, 2024–2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropower Project's construction progress and social-compliance posture, drawn from ADB's own project monitoring reports and two Accountability Mechanism complaints (2024 and 2025). # Key Data Points Installed: 140 MW reservoir-type plant River: Seti (Gandaki basin) District: Tanahu Owner: Nepal Electricity Authority Financier: Asian Development Bank (primary), co-financiers Main civil works: 140 m high roller-compacted-concrete dam (Package-1), underground powerhouse (Package-2), main water conveyance tunnel Status (FY 2024/25): ~67% complete per NEA annual report ADB monitoring visit: December 2024 — flagged buffer-zone demarcation, public-land acquisition and construction pace as open issues Accountability Mechanism complaints 2024 complaint: demarcation of buffer zones, free prior informed consent, land-for-land compensation, untitled-land registration 2025 complaint: filed 18 Feb 2025, deemed eligible for review 4 Apr 2025 # Relevance to Project Primary source for Tanahu's status, design basics and the unusually well-documented compliance issues that shape its completion schedule. The Accountability Mechanism filings are the reason Tanahu's delays are public and auditable — most Nepali storage projects lack that transparency layer. # What This Source Does Not Cover Dry-season energy contribution once the reservoir is in operation Plant-level cost escalation numbers (ADB figures redact subsidiary contractor detail) Coordination with downstream irrigation users on the Seti # See also tanahu-hydropower storage-deficit buildability

pages.title: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record
headings: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 900 MW Upper Karnali Hydroelectric Project — GMR-sponsored, repeatedly PDA-extended, and the canonical example of a stalled India-developed export project in Nepal.
pages.body_text: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record # Summary Reference compilation for the 900 MW Upper Karnali Hydroelectric Project — GMR-sponsored, repeatedly PDA-extended, and the canonical example of a stalled India-developed export project in Nepal. # Key Data Points Project basics Capacity: 900 MW Districts: Surkhet, Dailekh, Achham (karnali-basin) River: Karnali Design annual energy: ~3,466 GWh Sponsor: GMR Upper Karnali Hydropower Limited (subsidiary of India's GMR Group) Bid won: International Competitive Bidding, 2008 Export orientation: 88% of output planned for international sale Commercial / PDA history Original PDA deadline missed repeatedly since 2014 Supreme Court of Nepal limited extensions; allowed a 186-day extension to compensate only for time lost during its own interim orders Bangladesh Power Development Board signed a PPA with GMR for 500 MW in principle; MOU in April 2017 for 300 MW; PPA planned for July 2017; in September 2021 Nepal proposed only 200 MW to Bangladesh — none of these have closed Recent financing development (2026) Government of Nepal cleared a path for financial closure GMR submitted investment commitments: INR 65 billion from India's IREDA; NPR 20 billion from Nepali banks Equity restructuring brought in SJVN and IREDA alongside GMR; NEA retains its originally-allocated stake # Relevance to Project Primary source for Upper Karnali's PDA history, export structure and the 2026 financial-closure path. Important because Upper Karnali is the reference case — alongside arun-3 — for India-developed Nepalese hydro, but with the opposite construction outcome: Arun-3 is building; Upper Karnali has been paperwork since 2008. # What This Source Does Not Cover Bangladesh-side transmission readiness (Bangladesh can only import what a new Nepal–India–Bangladesh corridor can carry) GMR Group's own financial condition and its effect on equity pace The downstream-irrigation objections that a 2014 high-level committee raised and never fully resolved # See also upper-karnali hydro-geopolitics karnali-basin

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025)
headings: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025)
pages.excerpt: The World Bank's comprehensive economic assessment of Nepal with a significant energy/hydropower chapter. Provides the strongest international-institution perspective on Nepal's energy transition, trade patterns, and system constraints.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025) # Summary The World Bank's comprehensive economic assessment of Nepal with a significant energy/hydropower chapter. Provides the strongest international-institution perspective on Nepal's energy transition, trade patterns, and system constraints. # Key Data Points Hydropower capacity reached ~2,990 MW by end-2024 >70% of capacity added since 2018 came from IPPs IPPs accounted for 64% of total installed hydropower by 2024 >90% of hydropower is RoR or PRoR Trade shift: 2018 imported ~2,582 GWh / exported ~3 GWh → 2023 imported ~1,895 GWh / exported ~1,946 GWh Scheduled load shedding eliminated in 2018 76% of firms still reported regular power outages in 2022 >13% of firms reported sales losses above 10% from power cuts System losses: ~12.7% in 2024 September 2024 floods/landslides damaged 11 operational projects with >600 MW combined capacity # Relevance to Project Key source for the IPP-driven growth story, the trade transformation timeline, and the reliability gap (load shedding gone but outages persist). Codex [S6]. The firm-level outage data (76%) is uniquely valuable for the reliability argument in stranded-generation. Trade shift data central to data-trade-time-series.

pages.title: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment
headings: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment
pages.excerpt: Regional-scale analysis of the Ganges basin covering water storage, flood management, and transboundary dynamics. Provides the valley-shape storage paradox that Gemini highlighted.
pages.body_text: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment # Summary Regional-scale analysis of the Ganges basin covering water storage, flood management, and transboundary dynamics. Provides the valley-shape storage paradox that Gemini highlighted. # Key Data Points Aswan High Dam (Egypt, 111m): 162 BCM storage — flat terrain allows massive reservoir Proposed Andhi Khola Dam (Nepal, 110m): only 0.9 BCM — steep V-shaped Himalayan valley Karnali Chisapani proposed gross storage: 28.2 BCM, usable live capacity only 16.2 BCM Nepal's annual water flow through territory: 175–225 BCM # Relevance to Project The valley-shape paradox (same dam height, 180x less storage) is one of the most powerful visualizations for explaining why Nepal's geography simultaneously enables hydropower and prevents easy storage. Central to storage-deficit and data-storage-comparison. Gemini ref 18.

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022)
headings: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022)
pages.excerpt: Policy-oriented assessment of Nepal's power sector institutions and reform needs. Used by Hermes in Wave 1 as part of the source base for hydropower potential labeling and institutional analysis.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022) # Summary Policy-oriented assessment of Nepal's power sector institutions and reform needs. Used by Hermes in Wave 1 as part of the source base for hydropower potential labeling and institutional analysis. # Key Data Points Economically feasible potential: ~42,000 MW Institutional reform recommendations for nea and sector governance # Relevance to Project Provides the 42,000 MW economically feasible figure used alongside WECS numbers. Important for hydropower-potential-categories provenance. Hermes Wave 1 source.

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic
headings: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic
pages.excerpt: A water-sector-wide diagnostic covering storage requirements, irrigation, and the intersection of water management with energy. Provides the most authoritative estimate of Nepal's seasonal storage gap.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic # Summary A water-sector-wide diagnostic covering storage requirements, irrigation, and the intersection of water management with energy. Provides the most authoritative estimate of Nepal's seasonal storage gap. # Key Data Points Nepal's storage volume requirement to satisfy annual seasonal water demand: 29.86 km³ Nepal effectively has none of this required seasonal storage at national scale JICA estimates Nepal will need at least 1,993 MW of storage hydropower by 2032 (BAU) or 3,154 MW (high demand) Nepal's active dam storage: ~85 MCM (one of the lowest in Asia) # Relevance to Project The 29.86 km³ requirement vs near-zero actual storage is the single most dramatic number for the storage-deficit argument. JICA's MW requirements give a concrete planning target. Data feeds into data-storage-comparison. Codex [S7]. [!note] The 85 MCM active storage figure (Gemini/WB) and the 130-150 MCM total figure (Claude) likely differ in boundary — active vs total reservoir volume. Both are vanishingly small relative to the 29.86 km³ need.

pages.title: WECS Energy Sector Synopsis Report 2024
pages.excerpt: Covers Nepal's full energy balance for FY 2079/80 (~2022/23), including all energy sources (not just electricity). Provides the broader context for where grid electricity fits within Nepal's total energy system. This is the authoritative denominator for every "hydro share of tota
pages.body_text: WECS Energy Sector Synopsis Report 2024 # Summary Covers Nepal's full energy balance for FY 2079/80 (~2022/23), including all energy sources (not just electricity). Provides the broader context for where grid electricity fits within Nepal's total energy system. This is the authoritative denominator for every "hydro share of total energy" claim. # Key Data Points (FY 2079/80) Total final energy consumption: 532.42 PJ (−16.81% YoY vs 640 PJ in FY 2078/79) Consumption by resource class: - Traditional biomass: 63.87% (340.04 PJ) — fuelwood, ag residue, animal dung - Commercial fuels: 25.80% (137.38 PJ) — coal, petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG, ATF, furnace oil - Grid electricity: 7.23% (38.49 PJ) — up from ~4.96% in FY 2078/79 (+21.18% YoY) - Modern renewables: 3.10% (16.50 PJ) — biogas, solar, micro-hydro, wind Coal consumption down −41.50% YoY; petroleum products down −16.14% YoY Sectoral shares: - Residential 60.75% (323,463 TJ) - Industrial 20.91% (111,306 TJ) - Transportation 10.43% (55,553 TJ) - Commercial 5.04% (26,835 TJ) - Construction/Mining 1.92% (10,222 TJ) - Agriculture 0.95% (5,040 TJ) Hydropower capacity referenced: 3,349 MW installed, 9,940.86 GWh generated (FY 2023/24) Transmission system: 5,742 circuit-km at 66 kV+; 8,867 MVA substation capacity # Correction note Earlier wiki pages cite grid electricity as 4.96% of final energy. That figure was the FY 2078/79 value that appears in this report's historical prose. The FY 2079/80 headline figure is 7.23%. Both are correct for their respective years; the 2.3-point jump in one year reflects real electrification growth. Full cross-tab decoded at data-final-energy-mix. # Local PDF status [!warning] The file at in this repo is a 404 HTML stub from a failed download, not the actual report. The real PDF lives at the above (giwmscdnone.gov.np). Re-download before attempting any further text extraction. # Relevance to Project The 7.23% grid-electricity share — and the fact that hydro is only ~6.8% of Nepal's total final energy — is one of the most revealing data points in the entire project. It collapses the "Nepal is a hydropower country" framing into the more honest "Nepal has a hydropower electricity system inside a biomass-and-petroleum energy system." Central to master-thesis, domestic-led-hydro-strategy, and energy-substitution-pathway. Feeds into nepal-energy-profile and data-final-energy-mix. Codex [S5].

pages.title: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019)
headings: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019)
pages.excerpt: Site Inspection during Safeguards
pages.body_text: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019) Site Inspection during Safeguards # Summary The revised national hydropower potential assessment. Supersedes the older 83,000 MW theoretical figure with updated screening. Provides basin-level gross and techno-economic potential breakdowns. # Key Data Points Revised gross potential: 72,544 MW (down from legacy 83,500 MW) Techno-economic potential: 32,680 MW Basin distribution of gross potential: - koshi-basin: 27,805 MW - karnali-basin: 20,385 MW - gandaki-basin: 19,803 MW - All remaining basins: ~4,551 MW ~94% of gross potential is in the three major Himalayan basins # Relevance to Project The definitive source for hydropower-potential-categories. Only Codex [S3] surfaced the revised 72,544 MW figure — other providers used the legacy 83,000 MW. The basin concentration data (94% in three basins) is central to the strategic argument. [!note] The legacy 83,000 MW figure remains politically prominent. The 2019 assessment's 72,544 MW is the more defensible current number, and 32,680 MW techno-economic is the more honest planning baseline.

pages.title: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024)
headings: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024)
pages.excerpt: The 2024 master planning document for Nepal's river basins. Provides basin-level discharge data, seasonal flow profiles, hydropower potential assessments, and strategic planning frameworks. This is the most authoritative recent basin-level reference.
pages.body_text: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024) # Summary The 2024 master planning document for Nepal's river basins. Provides basin-level discharge data, seasonal flow profiles, hydropower potential assessments, and strategic planning frameworks. This is the most authoritative recent basin-level reference. # Key Data Points koshi-basin: 27,818 km² in Nepal; 1,826.5 cumec average border flow; dry-season flows reduce to ~10x lower than peak gandaki-basin: 32,148 km² in Nepal; 1,952.3 cumec average border flow; ~80% of rainfall in four wet-season months karnali-basin: 43,153 km² in Nepal; 1,255.9 cumec average border flow; highest hydropower potential per WECS 75% or more of river flow occurs in the wet season (June–September) Storage and multipurpose projects treated as central to moving value from surplus to deficit basins Babai explicitly described as rain-fed; West Rapti, Kamala, Kankai as water-deficit / dry-season constrained # Relevance to Project Foundation for Domain A (hydrology) across all providers. Hermes extracted it into working files. Codex cites as [S2]. Provides the basin-level evidence for seasonal-mismatch and claim-timing-not-volume. [!warning] WECS explicitly warns that atlas flow numbers are model-derived (MIKE SHE / MIKE Hydro Basin) and may differ from DHM observations or project-specific studies.

pages.title: Arun 3
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-25 River Longitudinal Profile of the Arun River
pages.body_text: Arun 3 Figure 6.3-25 River Longitudinal Profile of the Arun River Nepal's single largest project under construction — a 900 MW peaking run-of-river scheme on the Arun River in Sankhuwasabha, developed and financed by India's state utility SJVN. When it comes online (revised target: September 2026), it will almost double the country's operating hydropower fleet — but only ~22% of its output will stay in Nepal. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 900 MW (4 × 225 MW Francis units) | Status | Under construction — 74.6% complete (Dec 2025) | Type | Peaking run-of-river, ~3.4 hours/day at full output | River | Arun (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Sankhuwasabha | Developer | SJVN Arun-3 Power Development Company (SAPDC) | Cost | ~USD 1.6 billion | Concession | 30-year BOOT | Design energy | 3,924–4,019 GWh / year | Nepal free share | 21.9% (~197 MW) royalty-free | Evacuation | 400 kV DC line to Sitamarhi, Bihar | # Significance Arun-3 is the archetype of Nepal's India-developed export-first project class. Under the 2014 Project Development Agreement, SJVN builds, owns and operates the plant for 30 years, gives Nepal 21.9% of the energy as royalty-free share, offers another 10% on commercial terms, and takes the remainder to the Indian grid — a structure that makes Arun-3 the reference case when discussing hydro-geopolitics in the basin. It also changes the shape of Nepal's fleet, not just the size. Arun-3 is designed as a peaking RoR plant — 3.4 hours of full rated output per day, not continuous. So its 900 MW nameplate translates into a much smaller day-long energy contribution than its capacity suggests. That matters because Nepal's structural problem isn't capacity; it's seasonal-mismatch and firm-power deficits in dry months, which a peaking RoR plant can help with only to the extent the transmission system can absorb concentrated evening blocks. Compared to upper-tamakoshi — which is Nepali-developed, Nepali-dispatched, and continuous-RoR at 456 MW — Arun-3 is larger but strategically narrower: more export value than domestic reliability value. # Limitations & Controversies Repeated schedule slip. Original commissioning target was March 2025; SJVN has formally applied to extend COD to September 2026. Every year of delay compounds Nepal's storage-deficit exposure because Arun-3 is in the pipeline of projects NEA's planning has already counted on as "coming online soon." Evacuation dependency. The 400 kV cross-border line to Sitamarhi is only 58% complete as of late 2025. Without it, Arun-3's Indian export cannot move; with it, a single line carries most of the value. Both sides are exposed to the cross-border transmission asset. Free power as compensation theory. The 21.9% free share is the political justification for allowing the dominant share of the energy and revenue to accrue to India. Whether 21.9% is a good deal for Nepal depends on (a) the counterfactual — would the project have been built by a Nepali developer at all, and on what timeline — and (b) how the free share interacts with dry-season firm-power accounting, which Arun-3's peaking design constrains. # Official Visual References SAPDC's monthly progress reports are a materially better official visual source than the generic Arun riverside imagery that often gets used as a placeholder for the project. The February 2023 report cover contains a compact one-page schematic tying together the intake, headrace, surge shaft, powerhouse and tailrace alignment. Cover page from SAPDC's monthly progress report up to 28 February 2023. Source: SAPDC monthly progress report up to 28 February 2023 (PDF) # See also koshi-basin upper-tamakoshi hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation firm-power seasonal-mismatch inaruwa-purnea-interconnection — the 400 kV line being built to evacuate Arun-3

pages.title: Nepal–Bangladesh Electricity Trade Route
pages.excerpt: Nepal's first electricity export to a country other than India. A trilateral arrangement routed through India's grid — making India simultaneously Nepal's competitor, buyer, and logistics gatekeeper.
pages.body_text: Nepal–Bangladesh Electricity Trade Route Nepal's first electricity export to a country other than India. A trilateral arrangement routed through India's grid — making India simultaneously Nepal's competitor, buyer, and logistics gatekeeper. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Tripartite PSA signed | October 3, 2024 | Parties | NEA, Bangladesh Power Development Board (BPDB), NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (India) | First export | 12 hours on November 15, 2024 (symbolic) | Full-scale exports began | June 15, 2025 | Export window | June 15–November 15 each year (5 months) | Duration | 5-year agreement (until Oct 2029) | Current capacity | 40 MW | Agreed expansion | 60 MW from June 2026 (+20 MW, pending India's CEA approval) | Tariff | 6.40 US cents/unit | Currency | USD (unlike India trade in INR) | Route | Nepal → Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV → Baharampur–Bheramara 400 kV → Bangladesh | # Revenue Period | GWh Exported | Revenue | --------|-------------|---------| 2024 (12 hours only) | negligible | — | Jun–Nov 2025 | 147.43 GWh | USD 9.436 million (~NPR 1.35 billion) | # Projects Supplying Bangladesh NEA currently allocates output from Trishuli (25 MW) and Chilime (22 MW) — older NEA-owned plants — to the Bangladesh export quota. India's CEA has approved these specific 40 MW until October 2, 2029. # Expansion Trajectory Nov 2025 (7th JSC meeting): Nepal and Bangladesh agreed to add 20 MW (total 60 MW) from June 2026 Bangladesh's long-term ambition: import 9,000 MW from Nepal by 2040 Alternative routes under study: Barapukuria, Panchagarh/Thakurgaon, Bheramara, Comilla corridors through India Sunkoshi III (683 MW): Expert committee formed to evaluate this project specifically for Bangladesh supply Upper Karnali (900 MW): GMR-developed project had a planned 500 MW PPA to Bangladesh — status uncertain after Sheikh Hasina's fall (Aug 2024) # Strategic Significance 1. Buyer diversification. Reduces Nepal's monopsony dependence on India's IEX for export revenue 2. USD pricing. Bangladesh trade in dollars hedges against INR-NPR forex risk — significant given nea's NPR 2.5B forex losses in FY 2024/25 3. India as gatekeeper. Every MW to Bangladesh requires India's Central Electricity Authority approval and transits Indian lines — India retains structural veto over Nepal's market diversification 4. Seasonal limitation. Exports only run Jun–Nov, perfectly overlapping Nepal's surplus window — does nothing for dry-season revenue. The arrangement is complementary to the seasonal-arbitrage-trap, not a solution to it 5. Political fragility. Bangladesh's political transition (post-Hasina) has stalled larger pipeline projects (Upper Karnali PPA, direct transmission studies) # Related seasonal-arbitrage-trap — Bangladesh trade is monsoon-only, reinforcing the trap india-energy-relationship — India's veto/facilitation role data-trade-time-series — revenue figures hydro-geopolitics — trilateral dynamics dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the 400 kV link that physically carries this trade

pages.body_text: Bheri Corridor 400 kV Transmission Line A planned 400 kV evacuation corridor for the western Bheri system, centred in the annual book on uttarganga-storage-hydropower-project and the wider Sani Bheri IPP pipeline. In 2020 it was still a land, survey, and substation preparation story rather than a built line. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 65 describes the Bheri Corridor 400 kV line as a GoN-funded project intended to evacuate power from Uttarganga and other projects in the Sani Bheri basin, with a planned ~25 km 400 kV double-circuit line from Bafikot to Nalsingadh and a 400/132 kV, 200 MVA Uttarganga substation hub. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status in 2020 book | Under development / survey-stage | Core route | Bafikot (Rukum West) to Nalsingadh (Jajarkot) | Associated substation | Uttarganga hub, 400/132 kV, 200 MVA | Strategic role | Evacuation path for Uttarganga and Sani Bheri generation | # Why It Matters This is the transmission expression of the western buildability problem: large projects such as uttarganga-storage-hydropower-project do not have value without a corresponding evacuation corridor. The corridor sits squarely inside the wiki's claim-transmission-immediate-blocker logic and is one more example of generation ambition outrunning grid readiness. # See also uttarganga-storage-hydropower-project claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 stranded-generation

pages.title: Bhutan Hydropower Model
pages.excerpt: Bhutan is the most frequent regional comparison for Nepal's hydropower trajectory. The comparison is instructive and somewhat brutal.
pages.body_text: Bhutan Hydropower Model Bhutan is the most frequent regional comparison for Nepal's hydropower trajectory. The comparison is instructive and somewhat brutal. # Bhutan's Success Metric | Bhutan | Nepal | --------|--------|-------| Hydro contribution to GDP | ~14% | 3–5% | Hydro share of government revenue | ~26% | Marginal | Export price to India | $0.05–0.07/kWh | $0.03–0.05/kWh | Model | India-financed, India-built, India-integrated | Self-financed, fragmented, sovereignty-preserving | Bhutan's flagship plants (Chukha, Tala, Mangdechhu) were built with Indian grants and soft loans. India is co-investor with direct economic interest in project success. This gives Bhutan an unconditionally secure export market. # Why Nepal Chose Differently Nepal's more vibrant democracy, critical civil society, and strategic desire to balance Indian influence against Chinese investment led it to resist the "Bhutan model." Nepal opted for a slower, contested, fragmented development path to preserve economic and political sovereignty. This is a defensible policy choice, but it costs money: approximately $80–120 million per year in foregone export premium at current volumes. # Lessons Bhutan proves Himalayan hydropower can generate national wealth But Bhutan's model requires granting India near-total control Bhutan's projects have also suffered massive delays and cost overruns under Indian management Nepal cannot replicate Bhutan without fundamentally changing its foreign policy posture # Related india-energy-relationship — the bilateral context hydro-geopolitics — why sovereignty preservation matters domestic-led-hydro-strategy — Nepal's alternative path

pages.title: Budhigandaki Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-17 River Longitudinal Profile of the Budhi Gandaki River
pages.body_text: Budhigandaki Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-17 River Longitudinal Profile of the Budhi Gandaki River A 1,200 MW reservoir storage project on the border of Gorkha and Dhading districts, in the gandaki-basin. Considered a national pride initiative and the most important near-term storage intervention for grid stability. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 1,200 MW | Type | Storage / Reservoir | Dam height | 263 m (double-curvature arch) | Reservoir area | ~63 km² | Reservoir volume | 3.3 km³ | Base cost | $2.77 billion (~NPR 374 billion) | Total with interest | ~NPR 406 billion | Resettlement | 3,560 fully displaced families | # Status (updated April 2026) Moving forward — but still pre-construction. Jan 2026: Cabinet approved the investment modality (financial framework) — after years of repeated modality formation without approval Apr 2026: Bids invited for permanent camps and access bridge connecting Dhading and Gorkha — the first concrete construction procurement Land acquisition: ~90% complete. NPR 42.65 billion distributed as compensation for land, structures, plants and trees. 8,117 households in Gorkha and Dhading will be physically and economically displaced Financial modality: 70% debt / 30% equity. Government holds 80% stake, nea holds 20%, through Budhigandaki Company Limited Total cost with interest: NPR 406–460 billion (varies by estimate) Target: Construction start from January 2028, 8-year build — meaning earliest completion ~2036 Revenue projection: annual income NPR 31.48 billion; tariff split at NPR 12.40/unit (winter) and NPR 7.10/unit (monsoon) Generation: 3.38 billion units/year (1.41B winter + 1.97B monsoon) — crucially, 42% of output in winter, far better than RoR fleet ratios License term: proposed 50 years Chinese involvement: none. A previous proposal to award the project to China Gezhouba Group was politically blocked (Nepali Congress opposition). Project is now structured as a domestic investment initiative [!note] Contrast with Pancheshwar Where pancheshwar has produced zero concrete output in 30 years as a bilateral project, Budhigandaki is advancing (slowly) as a domestically controlled venture. The comparison is a data point for the domestic-led-hydro-strategy thesis. # Why It Matters If built, Budhigandaki would not just add generation — it would change the economic character of Nepal's electricity system. It addresses storage-deficit, seasonal-mismatch, and firm-power simultaneously. The revenue difference from shifting monsoon water to dry-season generation at higher prices makes storage an economic optimization instrument, not just infrastructure. [!warning] The project's repeated delays exemplify why buildability and governance matter as much as engineering. Nepal has no shortage of storage concepts — it has a shortage of executed storage. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The planned far-western Nepal–India interconnection, routed from Chameliya (Darchula, Nepal) to Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand). This would be the first 220 kV interconnection in the Mahakali basin and the gateway link for any future hydropower expansion in far-western Nepal
pages.body_text: Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV Interconnection The planned far-western Nepal–India interconnection, routed from Chameliya (Darchula, Nepal) to Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand). This would be the first 220 kV interconnection in the Mahakali basin and the gateway link for any future hydropower expansion in far-western Nepal (including the long-dormant pancheshwar prospect). # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (proposed) | Status | Planned | Nepal terminal | Chameliya substation (Darchula) | India terminal | Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand) | Primary basin served | mahakali-basin | Driving projects | chameliya-khola (30 MW operational), future Mahakali cascade | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Only far-western 220 kV cross-border. The existing Tanakpur–Mahendranagar 132 kV link is thermally saturated during monsoon; this is the upgrade path. Unlocks mahakali-basin. No large hydropower on the Mahakali basin has a viable export route at the current voltage; this line is the precondition for pancheshwar economics. Tri-country gateway. The Jauljibi side lies near the India–Nepal–Tibet trijunction; long-horizon planners have flagged it as a potential node in a broader Himalayan grid. # Limitations & Open Questions No construction schedule. Remains in planning documents; neither side has committed capex. Dependent on pancheshwar. The economics of a full 220 kV crossing hinge on the stalled Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project actually delivering. Terrain. Extreme high-altitude alignment through Darchula raises cost per km materially above Terai cross-borders. # See also chameliya-khola — the river and the existing 30 MW plant pancheshwar — the dormant anchor project mahakali-basin india-energy-relationship

pages.title: Damak–Godak–Phidim 132 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The eastern 132 kV evacuation chain that the annual book describes in three stages: Damak–Godak, Godak–Phidim, and onward Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli). It is the practical corridor through which the Mai/Kabeli-side hill projects were being tied back into the national system.
pages.body_text: Damak–Godak–Phidim 132 kV Corridor The eastern 132 kV evacuation chain that the annual book describes in three stages: Damak–Godak, Godak–Phidim, and onward Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli). It is the practical corridor through which the Mai/Kabeli-side hill projects were being tied back into the national system. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 45 states that the first Damak–Godak section was already in service, the Godak–Phidim section was completed and charged in 2019, and the Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli) section was commissioned on 6 August 2019. # Key Facts Segment | Length / note | ---------|---------------| Damak–Godak | ~35 km | Godak–Phidim | ~43 km | Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli) | ~13.33 km | # Why It Matters This is the corridor-scale version of the eastern medium-hill evacuation problem: relatively modest IPP clusters still needed a sequence of new substations and 132 kV links before their output could move. It is directly relevant to project pages such as kabeli-a, kabeli-b-1, and the broader claim-transmission-immediate-blocker argument. # See also kabeli-a kabeli-b-1 claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The west-central hydropower evacuation corridor — running from the upper Kali Gandaki valley (Dana, Kushma, Parbat) down to New Butwal. Built to pick up the cluster of IPPs in the Modi and Kaligandaki tributaries and tie them into the growing western grid.
pages.body_text: Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV Corridor The west-central hydropower evacuation corridor — running from the upper Kali Gandaki valley (Dana, Kushma, Parbat) down to New Butwal. Built to pick up the cluster of IPPs in the Modi and Kaligandaki tributaries and tie them into the growing western grid. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV | Status | Operational | Length | ~135 km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Dana (Myagdi) · Kushma (Parbat) · New Butwal (Rupandehi) | Commissioned | 2022–2023 (segmented) | Corridor ID | | # Significance Unlocks a trapped basin. Before this corridor, the Modi / upper Kali Gandaki IPP cluster had to evacuate through congested 132 kV toward Pokhara. Dana–Kushma–New Butwal gave the basin its own exit. Pre-positions for 400 kV. Terminates at New Butwal, the same substation that will host the mca-central-400 terminus and the gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. Significance for kali-gandaki-a's downstream cascade. The corridor accommodates future cascade additions in the basin. # Limitations & Open Questions Terrain risk. Myagdi-Parbat segment climbs steep, landslide-prone terrain; 2023 monsoon damage required emergency tower reinforcement. Still 220 kV. As the gandaki-basin pipeline grows, 220 kV may again become the bottleneck; a 400 kV overlay has been sketched but not scheduled. # See also kali-gandaki-a — the basin anchor mca-central-400 — the 400 kV it links to gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection — the cross-border at New Butwal

pages.title: Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: Nepal's first and — at the time of writing — only operational 400 kV cross-border link. Every exported MWh, including the bangladesh-trade-route allocation, transits this line. It is the linchpin of Nepal's current export strategy.
pages.body_text: Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV Interconnection Nepal's first and — at the time of writing — only operational 400 kV cross-border link. Every exported MWh, including the bangladesh-trade-route allocation, transits this line. It is the linchpin of Nepal's current export strategy. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV (operated initially at 220 kV, upgraded 2018) | Status | Operational | Commissioned | February 20, 2016 | Nepal terminal | Dhalkebar substation (Dhanusha) | India terminal | Muzaffarpur (Bihar) | Length | ~140 km (≈40 km Nepal + ≈100 km India) | Developers | Cross Border Power Transmission Company (CBPTC) — NEA + Indian JV | Financing | ADB, KfW | Current transfer | ~800–1,200 MW monsoon, ~300–500 MW dry season | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Export artery. Until gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection and inaruwa-purnea-interconnection commission, this is the only 400 kV door out. Roughly 95% of Nepal's 2024–25 export revenue moved through it. IEX access. Unlocks the Indian Energy Exchange market — the day-ahead pricing that has made monsoon exports profitable for the first time in Nepal's history. Bangladesh corridor. Also the Nepal-side leg of bangladesh-trade-route, which then transits through India on the Baharampur–Bheramara link. # Limitations & Open Questions Single point of failure. No 400 kV backup cross-border exists yet. An outage collapses export revenue immediately. Indian-side evacuation. Muzaffarpur terminus's onward capacity into India's Eastern Regional Grid is approaching saturation under peak Nepal export scenarios. Dispatch veto. Scheduling is set by India's NRLDC — Nepal cannot unilaterally increase flow even when physical capacity exists. # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — upstream feeder bangladesh-trade-route india-energy-relationship hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Dhungesanghu–Basantapur 220/132 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: KC-3: Transmission Line Stretch from Basantapur
pages.body_text: Dhungesanghu–Basantapur 220/132 kV Corridor KC-3: Transmission Line Stretch from Basantapur Chilime Hub GIS SS day view. An eastern Koshi-corridor buildout piece anchored on the Dhungesanghu and Basantapur substations. In the annual book it appears as part of the broader 220 kV Koshi corridor work needed to collect and move eastern hill generation. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 The appendix under-construction list names Dhungesanghu (132/33 kV, 30 MVA) and Basantapur (220/132 + 132/33 kV, 230 MVA) as active substation works under the Koshi Corridor 220 kV transmission line program. # Key Facts Substation | Capacity | ------------|---------:| Dhungesanghu | 30 MVA | Basantapur | 230 MVA | # Why It Matters The corridor is one of the enabling eastern-grid assets that sit behind arun-3-adjacent evacuation logic, even if it is not the final cross-border line itself. It is also part of the wider “collect before export” architecture summarized later in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 and structurally linked to inaruwa-purnea-interconnection. # See also inaruwa-purnea-interconnection arun-3 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 stranded-generation

pages.title: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-20 River Longitudinal Profile of the Dudh Koshi River
pages.body_text: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-20 River Longitudinal Profile of the Dudh Koshi River Project area of Dudhkoshi Storage A 635–670 MW storage reservoir scheme straddling Okhaldhunga, Khotang and Solukhumbu in the eastern koshi-basin — on paper, the single largest dry-season lever in Nepal's planned pipeline, and a decades-old feasibility that is only now approaching a real financing decision. NEA-owned, AIIB-financed-in-proposal, ~2027 construction start, ~2031 commercial operation if the schedule holds. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Total capacity | 635–670 MW (two design-basis figures in circulation) | Main powerhouse | 600 MW (underground) | Dam-toe powerhouse | 70 MW (ecological flow) | Dam height | 200–220 m | River | Dudh Koshi (koshi-basin) | Districts | Okhaldhunga, Khotang, Solukhumbu | Reservoir volume | ~1,581 MCM | Design energy | 3,377–3,444 GWh / year | Pumped storage component | 200 MW planned addition | Owner | nea | Financing | AIIB sovereign proposed | Schedule | Construction from ~2027; COD target ~2031 | Cost | ~USD 2.2 billion (NPR ~308 billion) | Displacement | ~162 households fully, ~988 partially | # Significance Dudhkoshi matters on three axes at once, and it is the combination that makes it structurally important: 1. Scale. 635–670 MW of reservoir storage in a system where the entire current storage fleet is 106 MW (kulekhani-cascade) and the only storage actually under construction is 140 MW (tanahu-hydropower). Dudhkoshi alone would roughly match the combined 2025 hydropower fleet of Nepal on energy volume and transform the dry-season firm-power picture. 2. Pumped storage first mover. A 200 MW pumped storage component is planned as Nepal's first "water battery." This turns Dudhkoshi into a grid-balancing asset, not just an energy source, and is the natural counterpart to the growing RoR fleet that drives Nepal's seasonal-mismatch problem on the daily timescale. 3. Climate-aware design. The project is being scoped with landscape stabilization and sediment management built in — a response to Nepal's sediment-as-design-constraint reality and to the fact that the upper Dudh Koshi routinely receives GLOF pulses from the Everest region (see glof-risk). Compare Dudhkoshi (670 MW, storage, AIIB, domestic) to arun-3 (900 MW, peaking RoR, SJVN, export-first). Both are in the Koshi basin; both are in the 2030 horizon; their strategic purposes are almost opposite. Dudhkoshi is the one that changes Nepal's winter. # Limitations & Controversies Four-decade gestation. The original feasibility was ADB-supported in the 1980s. Land acquisition restarted only in 2021. A project that has been "about to be built" for 40 years carries a credibility tax that any new timeline must earn against. Displacement footprint. The current figures (~162 households fully, ~988 partially affected) are material and will require a resettlement plan on par with anything Nepal has attempted. Social compliance challenges at smaller projects like Tanahu (tanahu-hydropower) are a warning about how much execution discipline Dudhkoshi will need. GLOF exposure. The 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled down the Dudh Koshi mainstem. A 220 m dam on a GLOF-exposed river demands a design flood that correctly accounts for cascading lake outburst scenarios, not a stationary climatology. How that design flood is set — and how conservative the spillway margin is — is the safety-critical question for the entire project. Financing latency. AIIB sovereign financing is proposed, not committed. The typical latency between "proposed" and "approved" for projects at this scale is several years, and Nepal will need to demonstrate fiscal space, guarantee structures and procurement readiness to move the conversation. # Official Visual References The new DKJVCL project material is a stronger visual source than the old bridge photos currently sitting in the top strip. The two most useful figure pages in the 2026 development teaser are the detailed dam-site layout and the basin study-area map. General layout of the Dudhkoshi dam site from the 2026 development teaser. Study-area and catchments map from the same teaser. Source: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project Development Teaser (PDF) # Related storage-deficit — what Dudhkoshi would begin to address koshi-basin — the basin context glof-risk — the 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled down the Dudh Koshi river tanahu-hydropower firm-power sediment-as-design-constraint # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory
