pages.title: Gandaki Basin
pages.excerpt: The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 verti
pages.body_text: Gandaki Basin # Overview The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 vertical meters — by some measures the world's deepest. This is Nepal's most developed hydropower corridor. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 32,148 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,952 m³/s (~61.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 74–80% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 22–30:1 | Gross hydropower potential | 19,803 MW (27.3% of national total) | # Hydropower Significance The most mature "buildable" basin. Hosts Nepal's early landmark projects — Kali Gandaki A (144 MW), Middle Marsyangdi (70 MW), and the kulekhani-cascade (on a tributary). The corridor benefited from road access (Prithvi and Siddhartha highways) and historical institutional familiarity. The proposed budhigandaki (1,200 MW) would be the basin's first major storage project and a system-shaping intervention for nepal-energy-profile. # Constraints sediment-as-design-constraint — the Kali Gandaki A plant is the definitive case study for turbine erosion. Suspended sediment spikes from 20 ppm (dry) to 50,000 ppm (monsoon). 65 million tonnes/year pass the dam site. More developed does not mean fully tapped. Significant untapped tributary potential remains. # Downstream Significance The Gandak system irrigates ~1.5–2 million ha in Bihar and eastern Uttar Pradesh. The Gandak Treaty (1959) gave Nepal only 15 MW from a major barrage — one of the most cited examples of treaty asymmetry. See hydro-geopolitics. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: Nepal's second 400 kV cross-border link, under construction. When commissioned it will double Nepal's 400 kV export capacity and break the single-point-of-failure dependency on dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. It is the direct complement of the mca-central-400 corridor, which terminates at
pages.body_text: Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV Interconnection Nepal's second 400 kV cross-border link, under construction. When commissioned it will double Nepal's 400 kV export capacity and break the single-point-of-failure dependency on dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. It is the direct complement of the mca-central-400 corridor, which terminates at the same New Butwal substation. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Under construction | Nepal terminal | New Butwal (Rupandehi) | India terminal | Gorakhpur (Uttar Pradesh) | Length | ~130 km (~18 km Nepal + ~112 km India) | Nepal-side developer | IL&FS / NEA JV (earlier) → India-side POWERGRID | Indian target | Commissioning in 2026 | Nepal target | Commissioning with mca-central-400 (2026–27) | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Redundancy for the eastern link. Full N-1 cross-border security for the first time once both operate in parallel. Western evacuation. Opens a direct export door for gandaki-basin and karnali-basin generation — which has historically had no outlet matching its latent capacity. MCA corridor payoff. The US-funded mca-central-400 spine has little export value without this link — the two projects are schedule-linked in NEA's integrated resource plan. # Limitations & Open Questions Schedule slippage. The Indian side has slipped repeatedly since the 2018 MoU; completion dates have moved from 2022 → 2024 → 2026. Nepal-side readiness. New Butwal substation 400 kV bays await final MCA corridor completion; misalignment of the two projects could strand capacity on either side. Tariff mechanism. Cross-border trading charges for this link have not yet been fully negotiated under the 2021 cross-border guidelines. # See also mca-central-400 — the Nepal-side upstream corridor dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the link it is meant to complement claim-transmission-immediate-blocker hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The 220 kV east–west spine across central Nepal, running through the Hetauda → Bharatpur → Bardaghat load corridor. This is the line that carried Nepal's grid before the 400 kV era — and still carries the majority of Terai distribution load.
pages.body_text: Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV Corridor The 220 kV east–west spine across central Nepal, running through the Hetauda → Bharatpur → Bardaghat load corridor. This is the line that carried Nepal's grid before the 400 kV era — and still carries the majority of Terai distribution load. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (double circuit) | Status | Operational | Length | ~180 km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Hetauda (Makwanpur) · Bharatpur (Chitwan) · Bardaghat (Nawalparasi) | Corridor ID | | # Significance Load-centre spine. Serves the Bharatpur–Hetauda–Butwal urban belt, Nepal's second-largest consumption cluster after the Kathmandu Valley. Redundancy for 400 kV. During HDDI outages this corridor provides a lower-voltage fallback for east–west flow, at reduced capacity. Tie-in for western generation. Connects Marsyangdi-basin and Kali Gandaki-basin output to the central grid before the mca-central-400 fully commissions. # Limitations & Open Questions Legacy capacity. 220 kV thermal limits constrain the amount of surplus generation the corridor can absorb; during monsoon peak, IPPs in the west are partly curtailed despite availability. Substation bottlenecks. Bharatpur's bus configuration has been flagged as a weak link in NEA's 2024 reliability review. # See also mca-central-400 — the 400 kV reinforcement dana-kushma-butwal-corridor — the 220 kV it links with in the west hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone

pages.title: Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa 400 kV Backbone
pages.excerpt: 220 kV Substation at Dhalkebar
pages.body_text: Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa 400 kV Backbone 220 kV Substation at Dhalkebar Nepal's first — and, until the MCA corridor finishes, only — 400 kV transmission line. The eastern spine that carries the country's aggregated hydropower output from central Nepal to the dhalkebar-muzaffarpur border crossing and onward to India. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV (currently operated at 220 kV in some segments) | Status | Operational | Total spine length | ~290–300 km | Owner | nea (Nepal Electricity Authority) | Key substations | Hetauda · Dhalkebar · Inaruwa | Segments commissioned | Hetauda–Dhalkebar (2018) · Dhalkebar–Inaruwa (2021–22) | Financing | ADB, KfW, World Bank (segment-wise) | Pane of operation | Central / Eastern Terai belt | # Significance Only operational 400 kV artery inside Nepal. Every MW of Nepal's export that leaves through dhalkebar-muzaffarpur transits this line. Aggregation point for IPP clusters. Evacuates the khimti-dhalkebar-corridor, Sunkoshi, Tamakoshi and (once online) arun-3 blocks toward India. Decouples load centres. Before HDDI, the Kathmandu Valley and the eastern Terai were weakly linked; HDDI re-wrote the grid topology. Partial 220 kV operation. Some Dhalkebar bays are still charged at 220 kV pending downstream 400 kV equipment — the rated capacity has not yet been fully unlocked. # Limitations & Open Questions Single-point of failure for exports. With no parallel 400 kV inside Nepal until the MCA corridor commissions, any HDDI outage collapses export revenue within hours. This is the operational form of the claim-transmission-immediate-blocker argument. Thermal margin tightens as generation grows. NEA has flagged loading rates approaching design limits during the 2024 monsoon peak; mca-central-400 cannot arrive soon enough. N-1 reliability not fully certified. Sub-station-level redundancy at Dhalkebar is the subject of ongoing reinforcement projects. # See also mca-central-400 — the parallel 400 kV spine dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the border link HDDI feeds inaruwa-purnea-interconnection — future secondary export route off HDDI claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The planned third 400 kV Nepal–India link, critical to evacuating the arun-3 export block (900 MW) and later upper-karnali-tier eastern generation. Currently in implementation-setup phase — the route exists on paper and in the bilateral MoU record, but construction has not begun.
pages.body_text: Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV Interconnection The planned third 400 kV Nepal–India link, critical to evacuating the arun-3 export block (900 MW) and later upper-karnali-tier eastern generation. Currently in implementation-setup phase — the route exists on paper and in the bilateral MoU record, but construction has not begun. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Implementation setup | Nepal terminal | Inaruwa substation (Sunsari) — eastern terminus of hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone | India terminal | Purnea (Bihar) | Length | ~160 km total (≈50 km Nepal, ≈110 km India) | Primary purpose | Evacuation of arun-3 to the Indian grid | Status driver | Tied to arun-3 COD — originally 2023, now 2027+ | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Arun-3 depends on it. SJVN's 900 MW arun-3 was approved under an export-first model. Without this line, its generation cannot reach the Indian market at rated capacity — a direct instance of stranded-generation. Third 400 kV cross-border. Combined with dhalkebar-muzaffarpur and gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection, it would give Nepal three east/central/west 400 kV export paths — the first real geographic redundancy. Koshi basin anchor. Establishes the first 400 kV link serving the koshi-basin directly, opening the door to future eastern storage (e.g. dudhkoshi-storage). # Limitations & Open Questions Schedule tied to Arun-3. Each slip in Arun-3 construction pushes the cross-border line as well — and vice versa. Environmental route. Alignment through Sunsari and Saptari districts still contested in local consultations. Indian-side approvals. Bihar state government concurrence and CEA tariff framework are the remaining gating items. # See also arun-3 — the project this line is built for hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — upstream feeder from Inaruwa koshi-basin stranded-generation

pages.title: India — Energy Relationship with Nepal
pages.excerpt: Energy Balance (GWh) in FY 2076/77 (2019/20)
pages.body_text: India — Energy Relationship with Nepal Energy Balance (GWh) in FY 2076/77 (2019/20) India is the decisive external actor in Nepal's hydropower future. Not just as a neighbor, but as the main practical export market, balancing supplier in dry periods, transit state for Bangladesh sales, and counterpart in major treaty-linked water and power projects. # India's Multiple Roles Role | Mechanism | ------|-----------| Primary buyer | Only viable large-scale export market for Nepali electricity | Dry-season supplier | Nepal imports 400–500 MW daily in winter at NPR 11.50–16/unit | Approval gatekeeper | Indian regulatory approval required for cross-border trade | Transit state | Bangladesh electricity sales route through Indian grid | Treaty counterpart | Koshi (1954), Gandak (1959), Mahakali (1996) treaties | Investment filter | India blocks Chinese equity/EPC projects from cross-border export market | # Treaty History Treaty | Year | What Nepal Got | What India Got | --------|------|---------------|----------------| Koshi | 1954/1966 | Flood control (shared); INR 19,200/yr rent | Barrage on Nepali soil; irrigation rights | Gandak | 1959 | 15 MW | Irrigation over ~50x Nepal's allocation | Mahakali | 1996 | 50% of pancheshwar output | 50% of output + flood control + irrigation | Net result | | 15 MW received after 70 years of treaties | Significant irrigation and flood infrastructure | # The China Factor India views Nepal strictly as its strategic sphere of influence. It has explicitly blocked purchase of electricity from Nepali projects with Chinese equity or Chinese EPC contractors, forcing Nepali developers to align with Indian interests for market access. See india-cbte-2018. # Bangladesh Diversification Bangladesh entered as a buyer (FY 2024/25: NPR 266.7 million initial export). But the route depends on Indian transit, Indian approval, and Indian grid. Bangladesh diversifies the buyer set more than it diversifies the corridor and political dependence structure. # Related hydro-geopolitics — the analytical frame seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the trade dynamic pancheshwar — the stalled mega-project bhutan-hydropower-model — the comparison case

pages.title: Kali Gandaki A
pages.excerpt: Nepal's largest operating power plant of any kind (144 MW), the Kali Gandaki A run-of-river scheme at Mirmi has anchored the national grid since 2002. It is Nepali-owned (NEA), ADB/JBIC-financed, and a useful foil to the newer India-developed export projects: it shows what a mult
pages.body_text: Kali Gandaki A Nepal's largest operating power plant of any kind (144 MW), the Kali Gandaki A run-of-river scheme at Mirmi has anchored the national grid since 2002. It is Nepali-owned (NEA), ADB/JBIC-financed, and a useful foil to the newer India-developed export projects: it shows what a multilaterally-funded, domestically-dispatched fleet backbone looks like — and also what two decades of wear do to one. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 144 MW (3 × 48 MW Toshiba Francis units) | Status | Operating since 2002 | Type | Run-of-river with pondage | River | Kali Gandaki at Mirmi (gandaki-basin) | District | Palpa / Syangja (powerhouse at Mirmi, Syangja; dam upstream in Palpa) | Owner / Operator | nea | Gross head | 115 m | Design energy | ~860 GWh / year | Actual avg output | ~592 GWh / year | Construction cost | USD 354.8 million (ADB + JBIC) | # Significance Kali Gandaki A still matters out of proportion to its size. At 144 MW it is only about 4% of Nepal's 2025 installed fleet, but it was for two decades the single largest plant on the grid — and in most years it produced more firm, dispatchable energy than any other asset NEA operates. It remains the reference point when discussing what a Nepali-utility-run, multilaterally-financed project can look like on the buildability spectrum. Compare kali-gandaki-a to two neighbours. upper-tamakoshi (456 MW, 2021) is larger, newer and also Nepali-developed, but pure RoR without pondage. arun-3 (900 MW, under construction) will be nameplate-bigger but peaking-design and India-dispatched. Kali Gandaki A sits between them in era and design philosophy — shared ADB lineage with Tanahu, NEA ownership like Upper Tamakoshi, and RoR-with-pondage shape that smooths evening load. # Limitations & Controversies The design-energy gap. The project was designed for roughly 860 GWh/year but has averaged closer to 592 GWh — ~30% below design. The ADB's own performance evaluation flagged sedimentation, variable hydrology and aging electromechanical equipment as factors. That gap is a real-world reminder that Nepal's nameplate capacity is not the same as its delivered energy, a distinction central to firm-power and seasonal-mismatch analysis. Rehabilitation overhang. In 2024 NEA floated a tender for design and supervision consultants to rehabilitate all three units. Any rehab of a fleet anchor plant introduces real derating risk during outage windows — exactly when the dry-season storage-deficit bites hardest. Timing the rehab around Nepal's import / export contracts with India is a non-trivial coordination problem. Pondage vs storage. Kali Gandaki A is not a reservoir project. The pondage it does have smooths daily load, not seasonal, so when monsoon flows fall off in January–May, Kali Gandaki A's contribution falls with them. Nepal's structural answer to that problem still sits in pages like dudhkoshi-storage, tanahu-hydropower and budhigandaki — not here. # See also gandaki-basin upper-tamakoshi run-of-river-hydropower firm-power nea

pages.title: Karnali Basin
pages.excerpt: The Karnali basin is Nepal's western major river system and the largest by area at ~43,153 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Bheri, West Seti, Humla Karnali, Mugu Karnali, and Tila. The Karnali feeds the Ghaghara, which contributes ~40% of the Ganges' baseflow at Varanasi
pages.body_text: Karnali Basin # Overview The Karnali basin is Nepal's western major river system and the largest by area at ~43,153 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Bheri, West Seti, Humla Karnali, Mugu Karnali, and Tila. The Karnali feeds the Ghaghara, which contributes ~40% of the Ganges' baseflow at Varanasi. This is Nepal's most dramatically underused basin — essentially zero large-scale hydropower despite having the best sites. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 43,153 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,256 m³/s (~39.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 65–72% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 15–20:1 (best stability) | Gross hydropower potential | 20,385 MW (28.1% of national total) | # Why It Matters Most The Karnali has the best dry-season flow stability of any major Nepali system (lowest peak-to-dry ratio at 15–20:1 vs 25–50:1 for Koshi). WECS calls it the basin with the highest hydropower potential. It is the most obvious strategic priority for future development. # Why It Remains Undeveloped Remoteness — western Nepal has historically had fewer roads, less political attention, and less financing reach Transmission gap — the grid west of Butwal relies on legacy 132 kV lines incapable of handling large generation Political neglect — eastern-centric development patterns systematically disadvantaged the west The proposed karnali-chisapani (10,800 MW) alone would transform Nepal's energy posture, but it remains conceptual # Climate Projections CMIP6 modeling projects the Karnali will experience 3–23% decrease in winter/spring discharge by end of century, alongside 18–51% increase in monsoon/post-monsoon discharge. Temporal concentration of water will become even more extreme. See peak-water. A useful basin-specific warning from ICIMOD: over 1981–2012, average precipitation in the Karnali study basin trended downward by 4.91 mm/year, yet discharge stayed broadly constant over the observed record. That is exactly the kind of rainfall-flow decoupling that makes annual averages misleading for energy planning. # Downstream Significance The Karnali/Ghaghara system serves ~2–3 million ha in UP and connects to Bangladesh's Ganges flow. See ganges-contribution. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Karnali Chisapani Project
pages.excerpt: The largest single hydropower project ever proposed for Nepal at 10,800 MW. Located on the karnali-basin main stem. Currently conceptual — requires massive downstream bilateral buy-in.
pages.body_text: Karnali Chisapani Project The largest single hydropower project ever proposed for Nepal at 10,800 MW. Located on the karnali-basin main stem. Currently conceptual — requires massive downstream bilateral buy-in. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 10,800 MW | Dam height | ~270 m | Gross storage | 28.2 BCM | Usable live capacity | 16.2 BCM | Status | Conceptual | # Significance If ever realized, Karnali Chisapani would make Nepal one of Asia's major power producers. It would provide massive storage-deficit relief, flood control for downstream India, and irrigation regulation for the Ghaghara system. The project also illustrates the wb-ganges-strategic-basin-assessment valley-shape paradox: despite the enormous dam, usable storage is only 16.2 of 28.2 BCM due to steep valley walls. # Why It Remains Conceptual Scale of investment far exceeds Nepal's fiscal capacity Requires India as co-investor and primary buyer Downstream bilateral politics (water sharing, flood control valuation) Environmental and social displacement at unprecedented scale See buildability and hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Kataiya–Kushaha 132 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The oldest Nepal–India interconnection still in operation — the historic first link that defined the Nepal–India power trade template. Originally energised in 1971 at 33 kV, upgraded to 132 kV, it has been through five decades of bilateral tariff and exchange-rate politics.
pages.body_text: Kataiya–Kushaha 132 kV Interconnection The oldest Nepal–India interconnection still in operation — the historic first link that defined the Nepal–India power trade template. Originally energised in 1971 at 33 kV, upgraded to 132 kV, it has been through five decades of bilateral tariff and exchange-rate politics. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV (originally 33 kV) | Status | Operational (since 1971) | Nepal terminal | Kushaha (Sunsari) | India terminal | Kataiya (Bihar) | Historic role | First Nepal–India grid tie | Direction | Predominantly imports into eastern Nepal | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Origin point of the Nepal–India grid relationship. Every later interconnection inherits contractual and technical conventions established here. Eastern Terai import backbone. Still the principal import route for dry-season supply to Saptari–Siraha–Sunsari, where domestic eastern generation is thin. Legacy accounting reference. Many of the bilateral power trade arrangements (e.g. the Radhika energy exchange protocol) were framed around this single link. # Limitations & Open Questions Legacy voltage. 132 kV thermal capacity is small relative to the region's modern load. During load growth spikes it has required load shedding or emergency procurement. Planned upgrade path unclear. Discussions to convert or parallel with a 220 kV link have not progressed beyond feasibility notes. # See also india-energy-relationship dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the successor 400 kV era hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Kathmandu Valley Underground Cabling
pages.excerpt: NEA's Kathmandu Valley underground-distribution program as it appeared in the FY 2019/20 annual book: a public-facing modernization project aimed at moving parts of the capital's medium- and low-voltage network underground while expanding associated urban distribution capacity.
pages.body_text: Kathmandu Valley Underground Cabling NEA's Kathmandu Valley underground-distribution program as it appeared in the FY 2019/20 annual book: a public-facing modernization project aimed at moving parts of the capital's medium- and low-voltage network underground while expanding associated urban distribution capacity. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 The annual book shows the program both as a ceremonial milestone on the opening pages and again in the project-management chapter, where cable-laying works in Chabahil are presented as part of the Kathmandu Valley distribution enhancement package. # Why It Matters It is one of the clearest examples of NEA trying to modernize the urban grid, not just add generation or long-distance transmission. It links directly to nea's distribution-reliability mandate and to the broader network-upgrade frame captured in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025. # See also nea nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 data-domestic-demand

pages.title: Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV Hydropower Corridor
pages.excerpt: The original "IPP-to-grid" line in Nepal. Built to evacuate khimti-i — the country's first large IPP (1996 PPA, 2000 commissioning) — toward the central grid at Dhalkebar. Its history mirrors the birth of private hydropower in Nepal and the long legal and political battles that s
pages.body_text: Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV Corridor The original "IPP-to-grid" line in Nepal. Built to evacuate khimti-i — the country's first large IPP (1996 PPA, 2000 commissioning) — toward the central grid at Dhalkebar. Its history mirrors the birth of private hydropower in Nepal and the long legal and political battles that surround it. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (double circuit) | Status | Operational | Length | ~75 km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Khimti substation (Dolakha) · Dhalkebar substation (Dhanusha) | Commissioned | 2016 | Financing | World Bank (IDA) — Nepal India Regional Trade and Transportation Project | Corridor ID | | # Significance First line built for an IPP. Before khimti-i, Nepal had no practice of building high-voltage lines specifically to evacuate independent power producers — the Khimti–Dhalkebar project was the template. Legal precedent. The project's land-acquisition lawsuits (2011–15) set Nepali case law on the public-interest use of private land for transmission. Feeds the export funnel. Terminates at Dhalkebar, which is also the origin of hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone and dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. Roughly half of Nepal's exportable hydropower still routes through it. # Limitations & Open Questions Capacity is effectively full. Contracted flow from Khimti-I + downstream IPPs exceeds 300 MW in peak monsoon; a second 220 kV or 400 kV reinforcement from the Tamakoshi–Khimti cluster has been studied but not funded. Sediment disruption risk. The corridor crosses steep Sindhupalchok and Dolakha terrain; landslide damage to tower foundations was flagged in the 2023 monsoon assessment. # See also khimti-i — the project it was built for khimti-ii — the sister plant that shares the corridor hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — the 400 kV it hands off to

pages.title: Koshi Basin
pages.excerpt: The Koshi (Sapta Koshi) basin is Nepal's eastern major river system. It drains ~27,818 km² within Nepal through seven major tributaries, including the Arun, Tamor, and Sunkoshi. The Arun is one of Nepal's oldest rivers — older than the Himalayas themselves, predating the mountain
pages.body_text: Koshi Basin # Overview The Koshi (Sapta Koshi) basin is Nepal's eastern major river system. It drains ~27,818 km² within Nepal through seven major tributaries, including the Arun, Tamor, and Sunkoshi. The Arun is one of Nepal's oldest rivers — older than the Himalayas themselves, predating the mountain uplift. The basin is the largest glacierized in Nepal (278 glaciers, 51 km³ ice volume) and carries the largest gross hydropower potential of any single basin. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 27,818 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,827 m³/s (~57.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 67–78% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 25–50:1 | Gross hydropower potential | 27,805 MW (38.3% of national total) | Glaciers | 278 (51 km³ ice) | Sediment significance | ~120 Mt/yr, nearly 25% of Ganges sediment load | # Hydropower Significance Largest gross potential of any basin in Nepal per wecs-hydropower-potential-2019. Contains the dudhkoshi-storage project (670 MW) and upper-tamakoshi (456 MW, largest operating plant). The Arun corridor hosts Upper Arun (900 MW, under construction). # Constraints Extreme sediment loads — ICIMOD framing puts the basin near 120 million tonnes/year into the Ganges system, or nearly 25% of the total sediment load. See sediment-as-design-constraint. Flood hazard — known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to catastrophic flooding and course shifts. The 2008 Koshi breach displaced 2+ million people. glof-risk — the 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled >80 km down the Dudh Koshi, destroying hydropower facilities. High hazard does not eliminate the basin's value, but it demands buildability filters on every project. # Downstream Significance Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar. Supports >40 million people downstream. Contributes disproportionate sediment to the Ganges system. Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Kulekhani Cascade
pages.excerpt: Repair works on draft tube during overhauling
pages.body_text: Kulekhani Cascade Repair works on draft tube during overhauling 6.6 kV Bus conductor maintenance Machine floor of KL1 Energy generation pattern of PROR/ROR and storage (GWh) Nepal's only operational reservoir hydropower system. A cascade of three plants south of Kathmandu. # Specifications Plant | Capacity (MW) | Type | -------|--------------|------| Kulekhani I | 60 | Reservoir | Kulekhani II | 32 | Cascade | Kulekhani III | 14 | Cascade | Total | 106 | | Combined generation in FY 2024/25: 279.8 GWh Storage volume: ~85–150 MCM (see data-storage-comparison) # Significance Kulekhani is the outlier that proves the rule. It is the only facility in Nepal that can shift water from wet to dry season for power generation. Everything else is run-of-river-hydropower. The fact that 106 MW of storage represents Nepal's entire reservoir base — against 3,400+ MW of total hydro — encapsulates the storage-deficit. # Related data-storage-comparison — Nepal's storage in international context storage-deficit — why this matters firm-power — what Kulekhani can provide that RoR cannot # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Mahakali Basin
pages.excerpt: The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and t
pages.body_text: Mahakali Basin # Overview The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and the subject of the 1996 Mahakali Treaty. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Average discharge | ~600 m³/s | Monsoon share | ~73% | Lakes in basin (Nepal side) | 16 | # Hydropower Significance The Mahakali Treaty (1996) entitles Nepal to 50% of pancheshwar's output (6,480 MW total, or ~3,240 MW for Nepal). After 28+ years, Nepal has received zero megawatts from this treaty. See hydro-geopolitics. The Mahakali already supports irrigation via the Mahakali irrigation system on the Indian side. # Strategic Notes The basin is less about sheer resource volume and more about the diplomatic relationship with India. The failure of Pancheshwar to materialize is one of the strongest examples of how hydro-geopolitics shapes — and stalls — Nepal's energy future. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Main Load Dispatch Centre
pages.excerpt: NEA's main system-operations node in Kathmandu, paired in the annual book with the Emergency Control Center at Hetauda as the backup facility for national grid dispatch and contingency management.
pages.body_text: Main Load Dispatch Centre NEA's main system-operations node in Kathmandu, paired in the annual book with the Emergency Control Center at Hetauda as the backup facility for national grid dispatch and contingency management. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 72 describes the main LDC in Kathmandu as the primary operating center and the Hetauda ECC building as the backup center for transmission system control during emergencies. # Why It Matters The dispatch center is the operational layer behind every corridor page: hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone, dhalkebar-muzaffarpur, and the rest only matter if NEA can actually control them as one system. It is one of the clearest institutional links between nea as owner and the real-time grid described in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025. # See also nea hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: MCA Central 400 kV Corridor (Lapsiphedi–Ratmate–Hetauda–Damauli–Butwal)
pages.excerpt: The second 400 kV spine Nepal is building — the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA-Nepal) compact's headline infrastructure, running from Lapsiphedi (Kathmandu Valley ring) through Ratmate, Hetauda, Damauli and terminating at New Butwal, where it feeds the gorakhpur-butwal-interco
pages.body_text: MCA Central 400 kV Corridor The second 400 kV spine Nepal is building — the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA-Nepal) compact's headline infrastructure, running from Lapsiphedi (Kathmandu Valley ring) through Ratmate, Hetauda, Damauli and terminating at New Butwal, where it feeds the gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. When complete, this corridor ends the single-point-of-failure problem on the eastern backbone. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Under construction (partial energisation 2025) | Spine length | ~315 km | Owner | nea (construction by MCA-Nepal contractors) | Terminal substations | Lapsiphedi · Ratmate · Hetauda · Damauli · New Butwal | Financing | US MCC grant (~US$500M corridor share) + Government of Nepal | Target full commissioning | 2026–2027 | Corridor ID | | # Significance Second 400 kV spine. Parallels hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone via an entirely different geographic axis (Kathmandu → Western Terai), providing real N-1 redundancy for the first time. Gateway to the second cross-border link. Terminates at New Butwal, the Nepal end of gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. Evacuates the western IPP cluster. Picks up output from the Marsyangdi and Kali Gandaki basins that has historically been trapped behind legacy 132 kV — partially solving claim-transmission-immediate-blocker for the west. Symbolic role. First large US-funded infrastructure grant in Nepal; its progress is read as a proxy for Nepal's willingness to diversify foreign partners beyond India and China. # Limitations & Open Questions Right-of-way disputes. Sections through Nuwakot and Kavre saw protracted community opposition over tower siting and compensation. Partial commissioning ≠ full rating. Early 2025 energisation covers the Ratmate–Hetauda link only; the full end-to-end 400 kV transfer capability awaits terminal substation completion at Damauli and New Butwal. Dependent on cross-border terminal. Full value is unlocked only when gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection also commissions. # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — the existing backbone it parallels gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection — the border link it feeds dana-kushma-butwal-corridor — the 220 kV it relieves claim-transmission-immediate-blocker

pages.title: New Modi–Lekhnath 132 kV Transmission Line
pages.excerpt: A western-hill 132 kV evacuation project aimed at improving supply in the Pokhara/Lekhnath zone while collecting power from the Modi-side hydropower cluster. In the annual-book snapshot, the associated Lahachowk switching and substation works were still under construction.
pages.body_text: New Modi–Lekhnath 132 kV Transmission Line A western-hill 132 kV evacuation project aimed at improving supply in the Pokhara/Lekhnath zone while collecting power from the Modi-side hydropower cluster. In the annual-book snapshot, the associated Lahachowk switching and substation works were still under construction. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 49 describes the New Modi–Lekhnath project as a 42.2 km, 132 kV double-circuit line intended to evacuate power from hydropower projects in the Modi basin and improve supply in the Pokhara area. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV | Length | ~42.2 km | Strategic load centre | Lekhnath / Pokhara | Associated substation in appendix | Lahachowk, 38 MVA | # Why It Matters This is the local transmission counterpart to the Modi-basin project cluster, including pages such as lower-modi-khola and upper-modi-a. It also shows how even relatively short 132 kV lines were carrying both evacuation and reliability roles at the same time. # See also lower-modi-khola upper-modi-a hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Mugu Karnali Storage HEP
pages.excerpt: At a proposed 1,902 MW, Mugu Karnali would be the single largest generation asset Nepal has ever planned — by itself larger than the country's entire current operating hydropower fleet. Still at pre-feasibility stage under state-owned Vidhyut Utpadan Company, open to public–priva
pages.body_text: Mugu Karnali Storage HEP At a proposed 1,902 MW, Mugu Karnali would be the single largest generation asset Nepal has ever planned — by itself larger than the country's entire current operating hydropower fleet. Still at pre-feasibility stage under state-owned Vidhyut Utpadan Company, open to public–private partnership bidding, and attracting expressions of interest from India's Adani Group and Bangladesh's Chowdhury Group. Its schedule (construction from ~2030, commissioning ~2034) and transmission dependencies place it on a longer horizon than any of the other top-10 anchor projects. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 1,902 MW | Type | Storage / pumped-storage reservoir | Turbines | 10 × Francis units | Gross head | ~285 m | Status | Pre-feasibility; PPP bidding underway | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | District | Mugu (registry primary); Bajura referenced in early materials | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Developer | Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUCL / VUC) | Design annual energy | ~5,883 GWh | PPP structure | VUC seeking 51% partnership stake | Schedule | Construction ~2030, COD target ~2034 | # Significance Mugu Karnali changes the arithmetic of Nepal's planning rather than the arithmetic of the current grid. At 1,902 MW and ~5,883 GWh of design annual energy, it is the one pipeline project whose success or failure genuinely rewrites the national storage-deficit and firm-power picture — more than dudhkoshi-storage, budhigandaki or any combination of smaller reservoirs in the current pipeline. It is also the project that most clearly surfaces Nepal's post-India export opening. Adani's EOI reflects the India-developer pattern that runs through arun-3, upper-karnali and west-seti. Chowdhury Group's formal bid represents Bangladeshi developer interest — the first time a Bangladeshi private group has bid for a project of this scale in Nepal. Whichever way the 51% PPP lands, Mugu Karnali is where the question "who builds Nepal's biggest reservoirs?" stops being rhetorical. Because it is storage / pumped-storage, Mugu Karnali is also the project that would finally give Nepal the grid-scale balancing asset the run-of-river fleet needs. RoR over-generation in the monsoon and RoR under-generation in the dry season are the two faces of seasonal-mismatch; a 1,900 MW asset that can time-shift energy across days and partly across seasons is the structural answer the country has not yet built. # Limitations & Controversies Horizon risk. Construction from ~2030 and COD ~2034 places Mugu Karnali outside the 5-year energy planning window most of NEA's current decisions use. Every dry season between now and 2034 has to be managed without it — by imports, by tanahu-hydropower and kulekhani-cascade augmentation, by demand response, or by accepting unserved energy. Transmission gap. A 1,902 MW generator at the head of the Karnali implies a new 400 kV (or higher) evacuation corridor through some of Nepal's most rugged terrain. That corridor is not in the current NEA transmission masterplan at scale. Without it, Mugu Karnali is stranded-generation by construction. Feasibility depth. The project is at survey / pre-feasibility stage with a two-year DoED deadline (extensible to five). None of the capacity, head or energy figures above has yet been locked down by a bankable feasibility study. Expect all three to move. Geopolitical visibility. Any bidder decision that confers control of 1,900 MW of upstream Karnali generation — and the reservoir operation rules that accompany it — will be read in Delhi and Dhaka. The downstream implications for Indian flood management and irrigation are material and not yet publicly modelled. # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit upper-karnali west-seti arun-3 hydro-geopolitics seasonal-mismatch stranded-generation

[!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 54 describes the Nawalpur substation as a 132/33 kV, 63 MVA node with additional 33 kV double-circuit line works to feed the local load area. # Why It Matters This is the kind of mid-voltage reinforcement asset that sits between the headline 220/400 kV corridors and the distribution-level service outcome. It adds detail to the broader story of nea trying to convert bulk power growth into local reliability improvements. # See also nea nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 data-domestic-demand

pages.title: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA)
pages.excerpt: Figure 2.1-2 NEA Organization Chart (as of September 2022)
pages.body_text: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA) Figure 2.1-2 NEA Organization Chart (as of September 2022) Timeline of Hydropower Development by NEA Nepal's central electricity institution. Simultaneously serves as primary generator, monopsony buyer, and sole grid operator — a combination that creates structural conflicts of interest. # Roles Role | Description | ------|-------------| Generator | Operates NEA-owned plants (Kulekhani, Kali Gandaki A, etc.) | Single buyer | Purchases all IPP output through long-term PPAs | Grid operator | Runs the Integrated Nepal Power System (INPS) | System planner | Coordinates dispatch via Load Dispatch Center | # Key Facts NEA and subsidiaries: ~36% of installed hydro; IPPs: ~64% System losses: ~13.46% (FY 2024/25) Eliminated scheduled load shedding in 2018 76% of firms still reported regular outages in 2022 Electricity access: 91.41% of households (5.586 million metered connections) — lower than the 99% claimed by previous management # Financial Health (FY 2024/25) Metric | FY 2023/24 | FY 2024/25 | Change | --------|-----------|-----------|--------| Profit before tax (NPR B) | 14.46 | 9.06 | −37.3% | Revenue from electricity sales (NPR B) | — | 125.27 | +8.46% | Total income (NPR B) | — | 138.87 | — | Total operating expenses (NPR B) | — | 97.79 | +10.15% | Electricity purchase cost (NPR B) | — | 77.10 | +11.73% | Total assets (NPR B) | — | 684.91 | +6.2% | Forex losses (NPR B) | — | ~2.50 | (yen appreciation) | May 2025 white paper (new MD Hitendra Dev Shakya) contested the previous management's claimed NPR 8B profit, revealing a NPR 5.26B loss and total liabilities of NPR 385 billion (including NPR 171.4B in long-term foreign loans under government guarantee). The discrepancy between the audited profit and the white-paper loss figure reflects different accounting treatments and periods — but signals that NEA's true fiscal position is worse than public-facing reports suggest. Debt Service Coverage Ratio: 2.27 (above 1.2 minimum — still comfortable) Return on Historic PPE: 4.50% (below 6% requirement) Debt-Equity Ratio: 47.59:52.41 # Institutional Issues Frequently dominates policy landscape, leaving the Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) sidelined Generation licensing not coordinated with transmission line planning Inter-agency disputes over EIAs, land acquisition, and forest clearance delay projects The single-buyer model limits market flexibility and creates PPA liability risk PPA liability exposure is growing: electricity purchase costs rose 11.73% in one year to NPR 77.1B, while export prices (the revenue relief valve) are falling — creating a structural squeeze between obligated payments to IPPs and realized market income Leadership instability: MD changeover (Ghising to Shakya, April 2025) resulted in dueling public narratives about NEA's financial health # Related stranded-generation — NEA's grid cannot absorb what generators produce seasonal-arbitrage-trap — NEA manages the import/export trade that produces value loss india-energy-relationship — NEA is the counterpart for cross-border commercial arrangements

pages.title: Nepal Energy Profile
pages.excerpt: Energy-specific profile of Nepal. On merge with the personal wiki, this content integrates into the existing entity page.
pages.body_text: Nepal Energy Profile Energy-specific profile of Nepal. On merge with the personal wiki, this content integrates into the existing entity page. # System Snapshot (FY 2024/25) Metric | Value | --------|-------| DoED operating registry | 3,791.874 MW (Apr 10, 2026, 189 plants >1 MW) | Separate DoED sub-MW list | 15.197 MW across 19 plants (last updated Jul 7, 2025; not the same cut-off date) | NEA annual report figure | 3,591.262 MW total / 3,389.912 MW hydro (FY 2024/25 cut-off) | Hydro share | 94.4% of the FY 2024/25 installed mix | Fleet type | >90% run-of-river-hydropower | Storage capacity | 106 MW (kulekhani-cascade) | System peak demand | 2,901 MW (Jul 1, 2025) | National peak demand | 2,409 MW | Monsoon surplus | ~850 MW exported during peak production | Per-capita electricity use | 370–465 kWh/yr | Grid electricity share of final energy | 7.23% (FY 2079/80) — up from 4.96% (FY 2078/79). See data-final-energy-mix | Net trade position | Seasonal exporter (since 2023); FY 2024/25 net exports: 699 GWh | Export revenue | NPR 17.46B (India + Bangladesh) | Approved export capacity | 941 MW (400 MW bilateral + 540 MW IEX) | Transmission | 5,742 circuit-km at 66 kV+; 8,867 MVA substations | System losses | 13.46% (NEA FY 2024/25) / ~12.7% (broader 2024 report framing) | Cross-border transfer capacity | ~1,141 MW approved | Household electrification | 91.41% households (5.586M metered connections); broader access indicators often round toward ~95% | Sep 2024 flood damage | 11 operational projects, 600+ MW affected | [!warning] Supreme Court Hydropower Halt In early 2025, Nepal's Supreme Court ordered a halt to development activities — including hydropower — within all protected areas. The ruling covers 12 national parks, 6 conservation areas, 1 hunting reserve, 1 wildlife reserve, and 13 buffer zones. IPPAN estimates this could affect ~300 hydropower projects and up to 20 GW of potential capacity. This is a significant new constraint on the "developable potential" claim. # The Core Paradox Nepal has crossed from chronic shortage into seasonal surplus. It now exports electricity in the monsoon and still imports in the dry season. The country has more electricity than it can sell and less than it needs — at the same time, every year. See seasonal-mismatch. # Reliability Transition The country is far better off than during the classic shortage era, but that recent success can be overstated if the timeline is forgotten: Scheduled load shedding ended in 2018. Before that, outages lasted up to 14 hours/day between 2007 and 2017. World Bank-linked framing in the merged notes puts the macroeconomic cost of that era at roughly 7% of GDP per year. That history matters because today's system should be read as a transition from shortage to fragile seasonal surplus, not as a finished reliability story. # Energy Beyond Electricity Nepal's broader energy system is not electricity-centered. Biomass still dominates. In the FY 2079/80 WECS balance, residential use was 60.75% of final energy, grid electricity 7.23% (up from 4.96% the prior year — a real jump driven by +21.2% electricity consumption against −41.5% coal and −16.1% petroleum). Hydro's share of total final energy is about 6.8%. Nepal's electricity story is more advanced than its total energy transition. Full cross-tab at data-final-energy-mix; strategic frame at energy-substitution-pathway and domestic-led-hydro-strategy. # Related data-fleet-composition — fleet breakdown data-trade-time-series — import/export trajectory data-potential-pyramid — from 83,000 MW to 3,591 MW installed data-map-inventory — merged map, layer, and figure catalog nea — the institutional center

pages.title: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP)
headings: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP)
pages.excerpt: 400 kV Tower at Koshi River
pages.body_text: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP) 400 kV Tower at Koshi River The World Bank-backed program that bundled Nepal's first large cross-border-capacity push with domestic reinforcement works. In the annual book's 2020 framing, NIETTP is the umbrella program linking the cross-border-exchange objective to both hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone and the hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor reinforcement works. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 66 describes NIETTP as a World Bank-funded project intended to establish roughly 1,000 MW of Nepal–India high-voltage exchange capacity while also executing the Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV line and associated substations. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Program type | Transmission and trade program | Core objective | Increase Nepal–India exchange capacity and improve reliability | Funding | World Bank | Linked assets in 2020 book | HDDI 400 kV works; Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV works | Framing | Cross-border trade + domestic reinforcement in one package | # Why It Matters NIETTP is the programmatic bridge between domestic grid hardening and the export-opening logic that later runs through dhalkebar-muzaffarpur and india-energy-relationship. It shows that Nepal's cross-border story was never just a border-wire story. The domestic substations and 220/400 kV reinforcements were part of the same reliability package. # See also dhalkebar-muzaffarpur india-energy-relationship hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor

pages.title: Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project
pages.excerpt: A bi-national mega-project on the mahakali-basin river forming the Nepal-India border. The largest proposed hydropower project in the region. Stalled for nearly three decades.
pages.body_text: Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project A bi-national mega-project on the mahakali-basin river forming the Nepal-India border. The largest proposed hydropower project in the region. Stalled for nearly three decades. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Total capacity | 6,480 MW (divided equally between Nepal and India) | Dam height | 315 m (proposed — would be world's largest rockfill dam) | Type | Storage / Multipurpose | Treaty basis | Mahakali Treaty (1996) | DPR | Drafted by India's WAPCOS (2016) | # Status (updated April 2026) Stalled. No construction has started after 30 years. Feb 2025: Energy Ministers Khadka (Nepal) and Patil (India) met in New Delhi; India "assured commitment to finalizing the DPR" — a promise made repeatedly since 2016. The headline "India assures yet again" was the literal Republica headline. 6th Joint Expert Group (JEG) meeting was to be convened by August 2025 to finalize DPR. No public confirmation it occurred. Core impasse: water/irrigation share. Nepal demands 50-50 sharing of irrigation and flood-control benefits; India insists on 75-25. Electricity is already split equally, so the fight is about the non-electricity value — precisely the multi-purpose benefits that make the project strategically important. Saptakoshi High Dam and Sunkoshi Storage projects face similar JTE stalls; the 17th JTE meeting was October 2023. Additional stalling factors: - India's internal federal politics (Uttarakhand and UP have conflicting interests) - Nepal's political instability (18+ governments in 30 years) - Financing uncertainty (initial phase est. NPR 336 billion; Nepal share NPR 126–130B) - Seismic zone 4–5 risk, with ~50,000 people affected across both sides - Environmental concerns including biodiversity loss and flood risk from a 315 m dam Nepal has received zero megawatts from the Mahakali Treaty after 30 years (signed 1996). # Significance Pancheshwar is the ultimate case study for hydro-geopolitics. It shows how bilateral treaty structures, domestic politics on both sides, and the difficulty of valuing non-electricity benefits (flood control, irrigation) can prevent even the most strategically obvious projects from materializing. # Related mahakali-basin — the river india-energy-relationship — the bilateral context hydro-geopolitics — the analytical frame Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung 400 kV Transmission Line
pages.excerpt: Nepal's planned first grid interconnection with China in the annual-book 2020 planning frame. The project is both a power-trade idea and a geopolitical signal: a proposed 400 kV route from the Ratmate side of the Nepal grid to the Rasuwagadhi/Kerung border axis.
pages.body_text: Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung 400 kV Transmission Line Nepal's planned first grid interconnection with China in the annual-book 2020 planning frame. The project is both a power-trade idea and a geopolitical signal: a proposed 400 kV route from the Ratmate side of the Nepal grid to the Rasuwagadhi/Kerung border axis. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 65 says the Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung line was being advanced as Nepal's first power-grid interconnection with China, with a planned ~70 km 400 kV double-circuit line from the Rasuwagadhi border point to Ratmate and a power-exchange substation at Ratmate. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Counterparty direction | China / Tibet Autonomous Region | Status in 2020 book | Detail survey and feasibility stage | Nepal endpoint | Ratmate | Border axis | Rasuwagadhi–Kerung | # Why It Matters This is the clearest 2020-era public example of Nepal trying to widen its grid geopolitics beyond the India-only transmission frame. Even as a survey-stage line, it belongs with rasuwagadhi and hydro-geopolitics because the point of the project was energy-security optionality, not just another domestic corridor. # See also rasuwagadhi hydro-geopolitics mca-central-400 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Tanahu Hydropower Project
pages.excerpt: A 140 MW storage-type reservoir scheme on the Seti River — the only large dam-and-reservoir project actually in active construction in Nepal, and therefore the single most important test case for whether the country can execute storage-deficit-closing assets at all. ADB-financed,
pages.body_text: Tanahu Hydropower Project A 140 MW storage-type reservoir scheme on the Seti River — the only large dam-and-reservoir project actually in active construction in Nepal, and therefore the single most important test case for whether the country can execute storage-deficit-closing assets at all. ADB-financed, NEA-owned, ~67% complete as of FY 2024/25, and shadowed by two Accountability Mechanism complaints over land and buffer-zone compensation. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 140 MW | Type | Reservoir (storage) | Status | ~67% complete (NEA FY 2024/25), Dec 2024 ADB monitoring ongoing | River | Seti (gandaki-basin) | District | Tanahu | Main dam | 140 m high roller-compacted concrete | Powerhouse | Underground | Owner | nea | Lead financier | Asian Development Bank | # Significance Tanahu matters less for its nameplate 140 MW and more as proof of concept — is Nepal capable of delivering a large reservoir project at all, on a reasonable timeline, with multilateral financing? Until Tanahu commissions, the only operating storage plant in the country is kulekhani-cascade (106 MW). Tanahu will nearly triple the storage fleet on day one. The strategic implication cascades upward. If Tanahu completes, dudhkoshi-storage (670 MW) and budhigandaki (1200 MW) become easier to finance: the next multilateral conversation no longer begins "Nepal has never actually built a large reservoir since 1982." If Tanahu fails — especially on the social-compliance dimension the Accountability Mechanism is reviewing — future large storage proposals will carry that memory as a risk premium. Compare Tanahu (140 MW, reservoir, ADB, domestic) to arun-3 (900 MW, peaking RoR, SJVN-India, export-oriented). Both are under construction but solve different problems: Tanahu addresses dry-season firm-power for Nepal; Arun-3 adds monsoon export capacity to India. Nepal's structural energy problem is the former, not the latter. # Limitations & Controversies Social-compliance complaints. The 2024 and 2025 Accountability Mechanism filings are the visible surface of a harder question: can a large reservoir in the middle hills be executed with buffer-zone demarcation, untitled-land registration, and free prior informed consent done properly? Current complaints flag all three. ADB accepted the 2025 complaint for eligibility review in April. Schedule discipline. 67% physical progress after ~10 years of construction is slower than comparable multilateral-financed projects elsewhere. Each additional year compounds NEA's dry-season firm-power exposure, since the entire medium-term planning rationale for Tanahu depends on it arriving before the seasonal-mismatch pressure peaks. Not a basin-scale answer. Even at full output, Tanahu cannot close the dry-season gap on its own. It is one node in a portfolio that would need Dudh Koshi Storage, Budhi Gandaki or similar scale to operate alongside. Framing Tanahu as "the storage solution" sets up disappointment; framing it as "the first storage receipt" sets up a reasonable expectation. # Official Visual References Tanahu's current public photo trail is still messy from a reuse and licensing standpoint, but the project overview deck circulated by Tanahu Hydropower Limited in 2016 does include two official visuals that are materially better than generic Seti River scenery. Project location map from the THL overview deck. General layout slide from the same deck. Source: An Overview of Tanahu Hydropower Project (PDF) # Related storage-deficit — what Tanahu begins to address buildability — Tanahu's progress suggests storage is difficult but not impossible kulekhani-cascade — the only current storage asset firm-power dudhkoshi-storage budhigandaki # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Tingla 132/33 kV Substation
pages.excerpt: A 132/33 kV, 30 MVA substation commissioned as part of the Solu corridor build. In the annual-book snapshot, Tingla is the bridge intended to connect Solu basin IPP output into the larger 132 kV system once the Mirchaiya–Tingla line is complete.
pages.body_text: Tingla 132/33 kV Substation A 132/33 kV, 30 MVA substation commissioned as part of the Solu corridor build. In the annual-book snapshot, Tingla is the bridge intended to connect Solu basin IPP output into the larger 132 kV system once the Mirchaiya–Tingla line is complete. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 45 states that Tingla substation (132/33 kV, 30 MVA) was commissioned on Bhadra 07, 2076, and identifies it as part of the Solu Corridor 132 kV transmission line project. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132/33 kV | Capacity | 30 MVA | Status in 2020 book | Commissioned | Corridor | Solu Corridor 132 kV | Upstream line noted | Mirchaiya–Tingla 132 kV double circuit, ~90 km | # Why It Matters Tingla is a concrete example of the smaller corridor substations that had to exist before generation in eastern hill basins could actually reach the grid. It belongs in the same “transmission before value” story as claim-transmission-immediate-blocker and the later nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 synthesis. # See also solu-hydropower-project solu-khola-dudha-koshi claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.excerpt: ERT survey at IkhuwaKhola HPP (IKHPP) Core Drilling at JawaTila
pages.body_text: Upper Arun Hydroelectric Project ERT survey at IkhuwaKhola HPP (IKHPP) Core Drilling at JawaTila An Upper Arun scheme in Sankhuwasabha that the FY 2019/20 annual book presents as one of NEA's major study-to-development priorities, with route access, geology, detailed engineering preparation, and 3D scheme visualization all still in progress. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Across book pages 113–116, NEA frames Upper Arun as a priority project with access-road work, feasibility optimization, project-location mapping, and draft engineering preparation still underway. # Why It Matters Upper Arun is a good example of how long the pipeline is between identifying a large Himalayan project and reaching bankable execution. It belongs with arun-3 and nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 because any future Upper Arun build would need both project delivery and corridor evacuation to line up. # See also arun-3 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 nea

pages.title: Upper Karnali
pages.excerpt: A 900 MW run-of-river scheme on the Karnali, licensed to India's GMR Group since 2008 and still in survey / pre-construction 18 years later. Upper Karnali is the canonical example of an India-developed Nepali hydropower project that has not been built: repeatedly extended PDA dea
pages.body_text: Upper Karnali A 900 MW run-of-river scheme on the Karnali, licensed to India's GMR Group since 2008 and still in survey / pre-construction 18 years later. Upper Karnali is the canonical example of an India-developed Nepali hydropower project that has not been built: repeatedly extended PDA deadlines, stalled financial closure, a Supreme Court that finally refused open-ended extensions, and a Bangladesh export PPA that has never actually closed. In 2026 the government cleared a new path for financial closure — the first credible forward motion in more than a decade. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 900 MW | Status | Survey / pre-construction (as of 2026, financial closure approaching) | Type | Run-of-river | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | Districts | Surkhet, Dailekh, Achham (registry lists Surkhet) | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Sponsor | GMR Upper Karnali Hydropower Limited | Design annual energy | ~3,466 GWh | Planned export share | ~88% | Licence awarded | 2008, via international competitive bidding | Equity (2026) | GMR + SJVN + IREDA + NEA | # Significance Upper Karnali is the twin case to arun-3 — same licence era, similar capacity, same India-developer / export-first model — but with the opposite outcome. Arun-3 is at 74% physical progress; Upper Karnali has been at "about to reach financial closure" for a decade. Reading the two projects together is how you understand the variance in Nepal's India-developed export class: the contract is not the product, execution is. The project also marks how hydro-geopolitics has widened in Nepal's hydropower conversation. The original export target was India, but a Bangladesh PPA has been live in some form since 2017 — 250 MW, 300 MW, 500 MW and 200 MW figures have all been quoted at different moments. Bangladesh is the canonical "second Indian neighbour" in Nepali energy strategy, and Upper Karnali is the project that has been asked to test that market opening first. None of the PPAs have yet actually closed. Finally, the 2026 financing stack — IREDA, SJVN and Nepali banks stepping in alongside GMR — is a pattern worth noting: Indian state-owned entities are now effectively taking equity in Nepali projects their original sponsor could not alone finance. That is a structural change in what "India-developed" means, and Upper Karnali is the first at-scale instance. # Limitations & Controversies Eighteen years without a turbine. The 2008 licence award predates the current nea grid code in some dimensions; deadlines have been extended repeatedly. The Supreme Court ultimately ruled against open-ended extensions, granting only a 186-day allowance for time lost under its own interim orders — a signal that the judiciary no longer accepts the PDA-extension cycle as routine. Bangladesh transmission dependency. Even if a final PPA closes, the 200–500 MW export to Bangladesh depends on a Nepal–India– Bangladesh transmission corridor that is still under development. Upper Karnali's export model contains an unresolved infrastructure prerequisite that is not on its balance sheet but is on its critical path. Downstream irrigation overhang. A 2014 high-level committee raised concerns about impacts on downstream irrigation in India's Ganga system. That objection was never fully resolved, only procedurally managed, and resurfaces in each PDA extension debate. GMR Group's execution posture. Unlike SJVN, GMR is a private Indian conglomerate whose project pipeline has shifted substantially since 2008. Whether GMR remains the right primary sponsor — now that SJVN and IREDA are in the equity stack — is an open strategic question that Nepal has not formally asked. # Official Visual Source Gap Upper Karnali is still unusually thin on stable public document figures. The Investment Board Nepal project and factsheet pages remain visible in search indexes, but the live page and factsheet download currently resolve to 404s, while the active GMR project page exposes basic project text without a reusable layout plate or site figure. This page therefore avoids generic valley scenery in both the top strip and body until a durable brochure, E&S disclosure, or layout PDF is recovered from the official source stack. Tracked official references: Investment Board Nepal project page Investment Board Nepal factsheet page GMR Upper Karnali project page # See also karnali-basin arun-3 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation run-of-river-hydropower mugu-karnali-storage-hep

pages.title: Upper Tamakoshi
pages.excerpt: Permanent Camp Facility
pages.body_text: Upper Tamakoshi Permanent Camp Facility Overall Project Layout Nepal's largest operating hydropower plant at 456 MW. Located on the Tama Koshi River in Dolakha district. Commissioned in 2021. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 456 MW | Type | Run-of-River | Gross head | 822 m | Headrace tunnel | 7.2 km | River | Tama Koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | # Significance Upper Tamakoshi is the plant that broke Nepal's load-shedding era. Its commissioning, combined with other IPP additions and improved import contracts, brought load shedding from 16 hours/day (2015–16) to near-zero by 2022. It represents ~16% of total installed hydro capacity on its own. It is also the archetype of Nepal's dominant engineering form: high-head diversion scheme with no significant dam. River drops 822 meters naturally; the engineering concentrates it through tunnels and penstocks to an underground powerhouse. # Limitations As a RoR plant, Upper Tamakoshi's output mirrors the seasonal hydrograph. It contributes heavily to monsoon surplus but cannot provide firm-power in dry season. It exemplifies why adding more RoR amplifies both surplus and deficit. See seasonal-mismatch.
