pages.title: Water and Energy Commission Secretariat (WECS)
pages.excerpt: Nepal's government planning body for water and energy. Produces the authoritative basin plans, potential assessments, and energy balances that form the data backbone of this research.
pages.body_text: Water and Energy Commission Secretariat (WECS) Nepal's government planning body for water and energy. Produces the authoritative basin plans, potential assessments, and energy balances that form the data backbone of this research. # Key Publications wecs-river-basin-plan-2024 — Basin-level discharge, seasonal profiles, strategic planning wecs-hydropower-potential-2019 — Revised national hydropower potential (72,544 MW gross / 32,680 MW techno-economic) wecs-energy-synopsis-2024 — Full energy balance showing grid electricity as only 4.96% of final energy # Role in Sector WECS handles theoretical planning while nea handles operational execution. The Department of Electricity Development (DoED) manages licensing between them. This fragmented institutional structure is part of the governance weakness identified across all providers. # Related nepal-energy-profile — the system WECS plans for hydropower-potential-categories — WECS provides the definitive numbers

pages.title: West Seti Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-2 River Longitudinal Profile of the West Seti River
pages.body_text: West Seti Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-2 River Longitudinal Profile of the West Seti River A long-stalled reservoir storage project in Sudurpashchim Province — the most prominent example of how Chinese withdrawal and project handoff have shaped Nepal's hydro-geopolitics. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 750–800 MW (varying by source) | Type | Conventional storage (reservoir) | Dam | 195 m concrete-faced rockfill, 433 m long | Location | Seti River, Bajhang/Baitadi/Doti/Dadeldhura districts | Affected areas | 16 wards across 8 local units | # Timeline of Stalling Year | Event | ------|-------| ~1980s | Project conceived | 2001–2011 | SMEC (Australia) held license; 17 years, limited progress | 2012 | Awarded to China Three Gorges Corporation (CTG) | 2018 (Sep) | CTG withdrew — cited geological issues, high altitude, resettlement difficulty. Rejected Nepal's proposal to reduce capacity from 750 to 620 MW | 2022 (Sep) | Transferred to India's NHPC Limited | 2025 (Oct) | Public hearings begun for Environmental Impact Assessment | # Current Status (as of late 2025) NHPC (India) is conducting EIA public hearings across affected areas Detailed project report is being updated An associated project — SR-6 (450 MW) — has been proposed below the confluence of Seti River and Budhiganga, in Doti/Achham districts, with a 207 m dam and potential 652 MW capacity No construction timeline announced # Why China Left CTG's withdrawal was not purely technical. The project illustrates the limits of Chinese hydropower engagement in Nepal: 1. Geological and altitude risk — genuine engineering concerns in far-west Nepal 2. Resettlement complexity — Nepal lacked a clear R&R framework 3. Commercial terms — Nepal proposed PPA payment in USD (unusual for Nepal), and CTG rejected a capacity reduction 4. Geopolitical signaling — India's sensitivity to Chinese infrastructure in border-adjacent areas created an unfavorable operating environment # Significance West Seti, alongside budhigandaki's history of Chinese-then-domestic pivots, represents a pattern: Chinese entities engage with Nepal's large storage projects but do not complete them. India then steps in (NHPC for West Seti, domestic for Budhigandaki) — but progress remains slow. The net result is decades lost on storage projects that would directly address storage-deficit. # Related hydro-geopolitics — the China-India triangulation storage-deficit — what West Seti would help solve budhigandaki — parallel trajectory of Chinese withdrawal, domestic/Indian pivot pancheshwar — another stalled storage mega-project, for different reasons

pages.title: Buildability
headings: Strategic implication: rank system value, not headline MW
pages.excerpt: Buildability is the first-order filter on Nepal’s hydropower future: a project is not “good” because a desk study assigns a large MW number—it is good if the entire delivery package remains credible after geology, hydraulics, hazards, and grid realities are stressed.
pages.body_text: Buildability Buildability is the first-order filter on Nepal’s hydropower future: a project is not “good” because a desk study assigns a large MW number—it is good if the entire delivery package remains credible after geology, hydraulics, hazards, and grid realities are stressed. WECS and licensing discussions note a persistent pattern: many licensed schemes remain stalled for years, not because Nepal lacks river energy on paper, but because paper feasibility ≠ executable project under Nepal’s combination of mountain geology, extreme sediment, slope access constraints, and thin EPC markets. # What buildability actually tests A serious buildability review chains interdependent risks: Geology and underground works — tunneling and cavern stability under high cover, fault zones, and variable rock mass ratings. Sediment and abrasion — turbine technology, wear life, and maintenance cycles are not O&M trivia when concentrations swing orders of magnitude seasonally (see sediment-as-design-constraint). Hydraulic transients and flood/GLOF exposure — hazard tolerance and intake resilience. Evacuation — generation without stranded-generation-grade transmission is incomplete infrastructure. Execution realism — contractor capacity, local logistics winter, and financing covenants that survive delay. # Strategic implication: rank system value, not headline MW Nepal should rank options by buildable system value: incremental dependable energy, loss factors, curtailment risk, and delivered cost—not by the largest number in a license. That reframing connects naturally to storage-deficit (seasonal re-timing is worthless if the dam never reaches COD) and to basin-scale complexity in karnali-basin megaproject corridors. Megaprojects such as budhigandaki are instructive case files: the controversy is not only “how big,” but whether the integrated package—reservoir, tunnels, communities, finance—can survive the decades-long path to operation.

pages.title: Domestic-Led Hydro Strategy
headings: Why domestic demand is not a small story
The constraint is not "can Nepal export?" — it can
pages.excerpt: Domestic-led hydro means planning the power system around Nepal's own load growth, reliability, and productivity — using exports to monetize surplus, not to define the investment program. It is the inverse of an export-first model that treats the domestic grid as a residual sink
pages.body_text: Domestic-Led Hydro Strategy Domestic-led hydro means planning the power system around Nepal's own load growth, reliability, and productivity — using exports to monetize surplus, not to define the investment program. It is the inverse of an export-first model that treats the domestic grid as a residual sink for whatever capacity IPPs can sell abroad. # The consensus across analytical lines Across multiple research threads in this project, the core strategic recommendation converges: Nepal should pursue domestic-led hydro with export support, not export-led hydro with domestic leftovers. The distinction is not anti-export; it is about sequencing and the objective function. Exports are valuable when they absorb genuine surplus at acceptable prices; they are costly when they institutionalize the seasonal-arbitrage-trap — cheap monsoon sales and expensive winter imports on the same national balance sheet. DeepResearch Part 7's "brutal answer" frames the current failure plainly: "The system is getting better at monetizing surplus, but it still pays a winter scarcity tax, and it has not yet built enough domestic electrification depth to turn hydropower into broad-based wealth." # Why domestic demand is not a small story nepal-energy-profile makes clear that "low consumption" is better read as headroom than as lack of need. Per-capita electricity use is ~370–465 kWh/year — roughly 5–7x below a middle-income norm of 2,000–3,000 kWh/year. Electricity's share of final energy is small (7.23% in FY 2079/80, up from 4.96% the year before — see data-final-energy-mix) — so electrification of cooking, heat, mobility, and process heat is a multi-decade demand builder. The specific sinks to target, ranked by volume, are worked out in energy-substitution-pathway. The sectoral breakdown (FY 2020/21, NEA) reveals a critical structure (see data-domestic-demand): Residential: 43% of electricity sales (~3,138 GWh) Industrial: 39% of electricity sales (~2,816 GWh) — nearly as large despite being a tiny fraction of customer count Industry is already the second-biggest user and the highest-leverage expansion target The electric cooking gap is the most dramatic underdevelopment: fewer than ~1% of Nepal's households use electricity for cooking. Biomass and imported LPG dominate. This is not a preference story — it is a reliability and appliance-access story. Shifting even 20–30% of cooking loads to electricity would create large, distributed, flexible domestic demand that simultaneously absorbs monsoon surplus and cuts the LPG import bill. Industrialization, urbanization, and replacement of costly fossil inputs add concurrent load. Highest-value uses include displacing captive diesel, electrifying cooking and transport, and feeding manufacturing where reliability converts kWh into jobs and tax base. Order-of-magnitude estimates suggest ferroalloys and similar electro-intensive industry can yield 8–10x the domestic value of selling the same energy as a commodity export to India at spot-like realizations. # The constraint is not "can Nepal export?" — it can Nepal is improving its exchange-linked value capture: the IEX price spread flipped positive in 2023-24 (Nepal sold at ₹5.61/kWh, bought at ₹4.43/kWh). But the whole-system average was still negative (sold Rs 8.72/unit, bought Rs 9.17/unit) because structural winter imports at peak-hour rates wash out the monsoon gains. The brutal constraint, per DeepResearch Part 7, is "whether Nepal can build domestic demand and system efficiency fast enough that exports become a high-value optimization layer, not the primary story." # Exports as complement, not compass india-energy-relationship will remain decisive for corridors, approvals, and timing. bhutan-hydropower-model is often invoked as a managed export reference — useful less as a blueprint to copy than as a reminder that host-country load and fiscal design shaped outcomes. Nepal's geography and politics differ, but the lesson holds: contracts and domestic anchors matter as much as megawatts. # Links to supply-side innovation When surplus is structural in the monsoon, domestic absorption can be engineered — not only through wires but through electrolysis and ammonia. green-hydrogen-nepal is the most ambitious demand-creation path: it turns seasonal glut into chemical industry and food security inputs instead of low-priced spill. # Related data-domestic-demand — demand distribution data, cooking gap, load profile seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the cost of not building domestic demand stranded-generation — monsoon surplus that domestic load could absorb green-hydrogen-nepal — industrial-scale demand from surplus twenty-year-strategy — how demand-building fits the phased strategy

pages.title: Energy Substitution Pathway
headings: The three dominant sinks, ranked by volume
Sink 1 — Fuelwood in residential cooking and heating (257 PJ)
Sink 2 — Coal in brick kilns, cement, and steel rerolling (34 PJ)
Sink 3 — Diesel, petrol, and ATF in transportation (55.5 PJ)
What this pathway implies for strategy
pages.excerpt: Energy substitution pathway names the specific question a hydro build-out must answer if it is to transform Nepal's energy system and not just its export ledger: which non-electric energy flows are being displaced, in what order, by what policy and infrastructure? Without this le
pages.body_text: Energy Substitution Pathway Energy substitution pathway names the specific question a hydro build-out must answer if it is to transform Nepal's energy system and not just its export ledger: which non-electric energy flows are being displaced, in what order, by what policy and infrastructure? Without this lens, "more hydro MW" and "more electricity consumption" can both rise while Nepal's overall energy system — dominated by fuelwood, coal, and imported petroleum — barely shifts. This is the strategic complement to domestic-led-hydro-strategy: where that page argues why domestic load matters more than exports, this one argues which specific loads to build. # Why the substitution frame exists The master-thesis treats hydropower as a conversion problem: turning hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, financeable power. But even a perfectly converted fleet only creates kWh. Those kWh become welfare only when they displace something Nepal currently burns. From data-final-energy-mix, Nepal's FY 2079/80 final energy is: Biomass 68.7% (340 PJ) — overwhelmingly fuelwood in cooking Petroleum 19.4% (103 PJ) — diesel, petrol, LPG, ATF, all imported Electricity 7.23% (38.5 PJ) — 94% of it from hydro Coal 6.39% (34 PJ) — 99% of it burned in brick kilns and cement plants Other renewables (biogas, solar, µhydro, wind) 3.1% (16.5 PJ) Everything outside electricity is a candidate substitution target. Ranking them by physical magnitude yields a clear priority stack. # The three dominant sinks, ranked by volume ## Sink 1 — Fuelwood in residential cooking and heating (257 PJ) The single largest energy flow in Nepal. 48.3% of all final energy consumed. Affected population: essentially all of rural Nepal plus significant urban pockets. Substitute: electric induction cooking, heat-pump water heating, electric room heating in winter. Why it's hard: appliance cost, grid reliability (evening peak 18:30–19:25 is exactly cooking time), LPG subsidy currently undercuts electric cooking on marginal-cost terms. Why it's the biggest lever: displacing 20–30% of cooking load creates ~50–75 PJ of new electric demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. That is bigger than any plausible export contract. Connected wiki pages: data-domestic-demand (the Biomass + fossil share of Nepal's final energy consumption. Today that share is ~92.8% (68.7% biomass + 19.4% petroleum + 6.4% coal − small overlap). A serious transition by 2040 should bring that share below 70%, implying ~25–30 PJ of net biomass+fossil displacement per year. That in turn implies specific quantities of induction cookers deployed, kilns retrofitted, EVs sold, and industrial processes electrified — all measurable, all currently unmeasured at the system level. If Nepal builds 20,000 MW of hydro and the biomass+fossil share in 2040 is still 85%, the build-out has failed on its own terms. If Nepal builds only 10,000 MW but the share falls to 65%, the strategy has succeeded. # Related data-final-energy-mix — the underlying data domestic-led-hydro-strategy — the strategic envelope data-domestic-demand — sectoral electricity demand today green-hydrogen-nepal — the coal-displacement pathway twenty-year-strategy — phased implementation seasonal-arbitrage-trap — what happens if substitution is not built claim-mw-not-equal-value — the value-capture logic master-thesis — the overarching argument

pages.title: Environmental Flow Policy
pages.excerpt: The IPSDP executive summary treats environmental flow and protected-area restrictions as buildability constraints, not as side notes. In the SEA mitigation table on page 37, moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows and m
pages.body_text: Environmental Flow Policy The IPSDP executive summary treats environmental flow and protected-area restrictions as buildability constraints, not as side notes. In the SEA mitigation table on page 37, moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows and must respect statutory restrictions in protected areas. # What the IPSDP says directly Hydropower can alter downstream flow, habitat continuity, and river-wide flow patterns. Where adverse effects are expected, project planning should refer to the IFC Good Practice Handbook: Environmental Flows for Hydropower Projects (2018) and build an explicit E-flow management plan. For projects inside national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, hunting reserves, conservation areas, or buffer zones under Nepal's National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act (2029/1973), prior permission is required. The same table says river modification and damming are not assumptions a developer can make inside protected areas; maintenance-flow and mitigation requirements remain in force. # Why this matters This is one of the clearest official signals in the current wiki that hydropower potential categories are not simply a matter of head and discharge. Protected-area law, river-ecology constraints, and mitigation hierarchy all cut directly into what is realistically developable. That makes this page a direct bridge between buildability, hydropower-potential-categories, and glof-risk: those concepts are all about why paper potential collapses when real-world constraints are applied. # Confidence boundary This page is limited to the policy and mitigation language stated in the IPSDP executive summary. It does not attempt to summarize Nepal's whole EIA or hydropower-permitting regime beyond what that table states. # Related buildability hydropower-potential-categories glof-risk moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025

pages.title: Firm Power
headings: Installed MW ≠ what you can count on in February
Potential pyramids are not firm-power statements
pages.excerpt: Firm power is dependable, year-round, dispatchable electrical capability under credible drought and system stress assumptions—not a branding label for “big projects,” and not interchangeable with installed megawatts, annual energy (GWh), or even average seasonal output.
pages.body_text: Firm Power Firm power is dependable, year-round, dispatchable electrical capability under credible drought and system stress assumptions—not a branding label for “big projects,” and not interchangeable with installed megawatts, annual energy (GWh), or even average seasonal output. Nepal’s public discourse routinely collapses these quantities. That conflation is not harmless: it steers policy toward headline capacity and away from scarcity months, which is exactly where seasonal-mismatch bites a RoR-heavy fleet (see run-of-river-hydropower). # Installed MW ≠ what you can count on in February A plant can be “online” while being strategically irrelevant for reliability if its energy is concentrated in monsoon weeks when the grid is already long—or if its output is volatile within-day in ways that the transmission system cannot absorb. Conversely, a smaller reservoir or import contract may contribute more firm value than a large RoR string, depending on commit rights, hydrology, and dispatch rules. The right planning question is therefore not “how many MW?” but how much electricity is deliverable when the alternative is rationing, diesel, or blackout—and at what cost. # Potential pyramids are not firm-power statements National “potential” figures sit at the top of a reduction chain from theoretical resource to what is financeable and buildable; see hydropower-potential-categories and the tabulated reductions in data-potential-pyramid. Those layers measure energy opportunity, not contract-grade dependability. Firm power is the bottom of the stack in strategic terms: it is what you underwrite, what you trade, and what industrialization plans should assume in dry weeks. # Analytical payoff Keeping firm distinct from big clarifies why winter deficits can persist even as installed capacity rises, why export discussions hinge on commitment windows not annual averages, and why system adequacy is a seasonal adequacy problem first.

pages.title: Nepal's Contribution to the Ganges
headings: Western basins as part of the same puzzle
pages.excerpt: Nepal occupies only about 13% of the Ganges basin area, yet contributes roughly 40–45% of mean annual flow and on the order of 70–80% of dry-season flow measured at Farakka-style accounting points in strategic basin literature. This hydrological asymmetry—small footprint, large l
pages.body_text: Nepal's Contribution to the Ganges Nepal occupies only about 13% of the Ganges basin area, yet contributes roughly 40–45% of mean annual flow and on the order of 70–80% of dry-season flow measured at Farakka-style accounting points in strategic basin literature. This hydrological asymmetry—small footprint, large leverage on timing—is the physical basis for Nepal’s upstream role in South Asian water security. # Underused abundance Nepal's problem is not that the basin is empty. In National Water Plan framing, the country has roughly 225 BCM of annual water availability but uses only about 15 BCM for economic and social purposes. The strategic issue is therefore conversion: storage, delivery, irrigation timing, and coordinated releases — not raw annual scarcity. # What the asymmetry implies downstream Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, and—through the concatenated regime—Bangladesh are sensitive to Nepal-origin contributions for: Kharif and rabi irrigation scheduling (canal drafts depend on low-flow stability). Flood peaks shaped by transboundary storms and sediment pulses (see koshi-basin for the extreme case). Drought resilience when monsoon spatial correlation fails. Aggregated planning numbers in basin assessments commonly cite ~5–7 million hectares of irrigated agriculture and ~200–250 million people as significantly affected by Nepal-influenced flow regimes (definitions vary; treat as order-of-magnitude strategic scale, not a census). # Climate change raises the stakes If variability intensifies and low flows become less reliable while extreme events increase, the marginal value of upstream storage, forecasting, and coordinated releases rises. Nepal’s geography could translate into bargaining power—but only if institutional capacity converts hydrology into credible, enforceable cooperation. Today, the more common pattern is leverage without durable agreement: episodic crisis diplomacy rather than rules-based benefit sharing. # Geopolitics and national planning The strategic read is developed in parallel on hydro-geopolitics and india-energy-relationship: electrons and cubic meters are joint products in peoples’ minds even when markets separate them. Nepal’s National Water Plan discourse (see national-water-plan-nepal) is the domestic mirror—attempting to align IWRM, energy, and disaster risk under one national narrative. # Western basins as part of the same puzzle karnali-basin feeds the Ghaghara complex and therefore UP’s vast irrigated plains. Storage and diversion debates there are not “local” Nepali stories; they are subcontinental stories with Nepali headwaters at the center. # Analytical bottom line Nepal’s contribution to the Ganges is not proportional to its land share. That fact creates real upstream leverage—and also moral hazard if Nepal builds unilateral infrastructure that ignores downstream sensitivities. The research task is to map where cooperation creates surplus for both sides versus where zero-sum framing is unavoidable.

pages.title: GLOF Risk
headings: Process: from lake instability to basin-scale impact
A concrete 2024 illustration: Thame / Dudh Koshi
Implications for hydropower design and siting
pages.excerpt: Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are rapid releases of water from moraine-dammed or ice-dammed lakes, often triggered by avalanches, seismicity, or hydrostatic/overpressure failure. In Nepal’s context, they are a first-order constraint on how confidently one can site run-of-r
pages.body_text: GLOF Risk Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are rapid releases of water from moraine-dammed or ice-dammed lakes, often triggered by avalanches, seismicity, or hydrostatic/overpressure failure. In Nepal’s context, they are a first-order constraint on how confidently one can site run-of-river corridors, headworks, and storage in transboundary basins—because a GLOF is not a “flood” in the sense of a predictable monsoon stage; it can arrive as a high-energy sediment slurry with invented hydrographs that exceed historical design bases. # Process: from lake instability to basin-scale impact Glacier retreat creates and enlarges supraglacial and proglacial lakes, frequently impounded by unconsolidated moraines with weak spillway geometry. When the impoundment fails, the release can cascade: one lake drains into another valley reach, remobilizing sediment, eroding channels, and amplifying peak discharge far downstream. Flow paths can extend hundreds of kilometers, affecting tributaries and mainstems long after the originating valley. # A concrete 2024 illustration: Thame / Dudh Koshi The 2024 Thame Valley sequence—where a rock avalanche impacted Upper Ngole Cho, then contributed to failure dynamics affecting Lower Ngole Cho, producing on the order of a ~22 m head drop through a ~51 m breach width (figures as reported in rapid assessments)—generated a hyper-concentrated flow tracked >80 km down the Thame and Dudh Koshi systems, damaging settlements and hydropower works. The event is a textbook case for why stationary hydrology assumptions are inadequate: the initiating process was mass movement + lake chain, not a monsoon exceedance. # Implications for hydropower design and siting Planned RoR and storage projects along GLOF-prone corridors face three intertwined issues: 1. Hydraulic load cases that historically used gauge-based floods may understate tail scenarios driven by sudden moraine breaches and sediment bulking. 2. Sediment pulses from GLOFs connect hazard to turbine wear, intake burial, and channel avulsion—overlapping with sediment-as-design-constraint in basins like koshi-basin. 3. Insurance, financing, and EPC risk feed back into buildability: projects that look attractive on LCOE spreadsheets can fail non-market tests once tail risks are priced honestly. Storage concepts in high-hazard reaches—dudhkoshi-storage is a prominent example—must therefore integrate landscape stabilization, monitoring, and cascading-failure scenarios, not only energy-yield optimization. # Link to the glacio-hydrological arc The same deglaciation dynamics that define the peak-water window also produce unstable lakes. Mitigation (lowering, controlled drainage, early warning) competes for fiscal and institutional bandwidth with generation targets. Treating GLOFs as a side environmental issue is a category error: they are infrastructure solvency issues.

pages.title: Green Hydrogen Nepal
headings: Why fertilizer is the natural first anchor
pages.excerpt: Green hydrogen in Nepal is best understood as a bridge technology between surplus monsoon electricity and hard-to-electrify domestic industry—not as a export commodity fantasy disconnected from grid physics. Electrolysis converts low-utilization wet-season kWh into storable molec
pages.body_text: Green Hydrogen Nepal Green hydrogen in Nepal is best understood as a bridge technology between surplus monsoon electricity and hard-to-electrify domestic industry—not as a export commodity fantasy disconnected from grid physics. Electrolysis converts low-utilization wet-season kWh into storable molecules (hydrogen, then ammonia or other carriers), reshaping the economics of seasonal-arbitrage-trap by absorbing energy when the system is long and delivering products when the economy needs them. # Why fertilizer is the natural first anchor Nepal imports essentially all chemical fertilizer; food security and farm productivity are therefore foreign-exchange and supply-chain dependent. A domestic ammonia pathway—hydrogen → Haber-Bosch → urea/ammonium products—ties directly to sovereignty and base-load demand. Order-of-magnitude sector sketches in national roadmaps suggest a modern fertilizer complex can present hundreds of megawatts of baseload-class demand (commonly cited ~500 MW in policy-grade scenarios—exact sizing is plant-design specific). That scale matters: it is large enough to change dispatch rather than decorate it. # Cement and other industrial offtake Cement decarbonization is another hydrogen sink: replacing fossil fuel combustion in clinker/pyroprocessing with hydrogen or hybrid fuels stacks up large annual H₂ demand in national modeling discourse (illustrative national figures on the order of ~228,000 tonnes H₂/year implying ~2 GW of electrolysis capacity are used in roadmap arithmetic—treat as planning magnitude, not a signed FEED package). The point for domestic-led-hydro-strategy is structural: these are domestic value chains that pay for reliability differently than spot export. # Policy trajectory The Green Hydrogen Policy (2024) signals official intent to coordinate standards, pilots, incentives, and safety. The typical staged narrative runs pilots 2025–2027 toward industrial scale by ~2035, with cost projections in policy documents clustering around ~$2.15/kg by mid-2030s under favorable renewable input cost paths—again, a directional planning anchor. # Relationship to curtailment and surplus If new RoR continues to outpace demand and storage, stranded-generation risk rises: energy with nowhere to go at non-trivial variable cost. Electrolysis is one of the few scalable sinks that can ride renewable variability if system integration (oxygen use, water treatment, grid connection, industrial symbiosis) is solved. Detailed citations and program structure belong in green-hydrogen-roadmap-nepal.

pages.title: Hydro-Geopolitics
headings: Downstream scale: why Nepal’s choices ripple
India as decisive actor; China as absent partner
pages.excerpt: Hydro-geopolitics is the lens that treats Nepal’s rivers as co-produced by geology, monsoon, ice, and downstream human systems—and therefore as objects of bargaining, not merely as engineering inputs. Electricity is one slice of a larger portfolio that includes irrigation, flood
pages.body_text: Hydro-Geopolitics Hydro-geopolitics is the lens that treats Nepal’s rivers as co-produced by geology, monsoon, ice, and downstream human systems—and therefore as objects of bargaining, not merely as engineering inputs. Electricity is one slice of a larger portfolio that includes irrigation, flood management, navigation where relevant, ecosystem services, and disaster risk. Ignoring this layer turns technically “optimal” projects into politically unrealizable ones. # Downstream scale: why Nepal’s choices ripple Nepal-origin rivers contribute a large share of Ganges annual discharge (commonly cited ~40–45%) and an even larger share of dry-season flow (~70–80% in Farakka-style accounting used in basin assessments). Those percentages imply hundreds of millions of people and many million hectares of irrigated command indirectly indexed to Nepal-influenced regimes. Illustrative orders of magnitude appear repeatedly in basin literature: Koshi irrigation on the order of 500–600 thousand hectares in Bihar; Gandak systems on the order of 1.5–2 million hectares across Bihar/UP; Karnali/Ghaghara complexes affecting roughly 2–3 million hectares in UP. The exact numbers shift with dataset and boundary definition; the strategic fact is extreme downstream dependence on upstream timing and quality of flows—developed analytically in ganges-contribution. # Treaties, asymmetry, and unfinished business Historical agreements (Koshi, Gandak, Mahakali) are often described as asymmetric in implementation experience: formal principles of equity and benefit sharing collide with as-built financing, maintenance, and interpretation disputes. pancheshwar remains the emblematic large joint project whose stall shows how sovereignty, resettlement, benefit splits, and India’s internal federal politics can freeze decades of studies into zero concrete storage. # India as decisive actor; China as absent partner india-energy-relationship is decisive for cross-border trade, transmission corridors, grid codes, and perceptions of security. China's role in Nepal hydro has been receding, not growing: China Three Gorges withdrew from west-seti in 2018; China Gezhouba was blocked from budhigandaki on political grounds. No major Chinese-linked storage project is currently under active construction in Nepal. India, meanwhile, has expanded its presence: NHPC took over West Seti (2022), SJVN is building the 900 MW Arun-3, and India's Power Grid Corporation agreed in April 2025 to build high-capacity cross-border transmission. The triangulation is not symmetric: India can veto or complicate Chinese-backed projects through market access, transmission denial, and geopolitical pressure, while China has no equivalent lever over India-backed schemes. Nepal's ability to play one against the other is limited in practice — a finding that tempers the "strategic leverage" narrative. # Bhutan as comparator, not clone bhutan-hydropower-model illustrates how export-led hydro can work when fiscal rules, load management, and a patron relationship align. Nepal’s larger population, internal political fragmentation, and different downstream co-riparian geometry mean copy-paste is misleading—but the comparison clarifies that governance is where opportunity is won or lost. # Basin anchors Transboundary technical narratives often anchor on koshi-basin as the hazard–sediment–irrigation nexus par excellence. Hydro-geopolitics is therefore not a “soft” add-on to power-sector economics; it is frequently the binding constraint on what can be built, financed, and operated.

pages.title: Hydropower Potential Categories
pages.excerpt: “Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power).
pages.body_text: Hydropower Potential Categories “Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power). # The reduction pyramid (conceptual chain) Think of a pyramid—quantified in data-potential-pyramid—where each step applies filters the previous step ignores: 1. Theoretical / gross potential — legacy national figures near ~83,000 MW appear in older discourse; WECS (2019) reframed theoretical potential around ~72,500 MW depending on methodological choices. 2. Technical potential — what is physically plausible with conventional technology; commonly discussed in the ~42,000–46,000 MW range in national studies (exact bounds depend on site database and assumptions). 3. Techno-economic potential — WECS (2019) reports 32,680 MW at this layer: sites that meet engineering and economic screening rules as modeled. 4. Economically viable (market window) — often discussed as ~25,000–35,000 MW under price and financing assumptions that move with interest rates, carbon policy, and India import terms. 5. Realistically developable by ~2040 — planning horizons frequently land around ~10,000–15,000 MW once execution risk, transmission, and social license are acknowledged. 6. Installed — roughly ~3,591 MW hydro installed (order-of-magnitude anchor for “today”). These categories are not interchangeable. Moving down the chain is not “being conservative”; it is changing the question. # Basin concentration A large share of gross potential concentrates in three major basins—koshi-basin, karnali-basin, and gandaki-basin—often cited on the order of ~94% of national potential. That concentration matters for cascade externalities, sediment, and geopolitical export corridors: “national potential” is geographically lumpy. # Why this matters for analysis Potential-category discipline prevents two common failures: inflating creditworthiness with theoretical MW, and confusing annual energy with dry-season dependability. Primary methodology and citations live under wecs-hydropower-potential-2019.

pages.title: Nepal Power Trade Schemes
headings: Generator-facing wholesale channels named in the IPSDP
Commercial modes layered on top of those channels
Why this matters to the wiki
pages.excerpt: In Section 2.4 of moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, the IPSDP does two different but related things. It names three generator-facing wholesale customer categories, and it separately describes the commercial modes those channels may use, including exchange-market sales and long-term
pages.body_text: Nepal Power Trade Schemes In Section 2.4 of moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, the IPSDP does two different but related things. It names three generator-facing wholesale customer categories, and it separately describes the commercial modes those channels may use, including exchange-market sales and long-term PPAs. # Generator-facing wholesale channels named in the IPSDP Channel | Buyer | How the PDF frames it | ---------|-------|------------------------| NEA default | nea | Conventional domestic trade. NEA buys power, serves the domestic market, and exports surplus itself. Lowest administrative burden for smaller projects. | Power-trading company | Nepal trading company such as NPTCL | A future channel in which trading companies intermediate between generators and markets. The PDF explicitly says sales may be either PPA-based or market-linked. | Direct overseas trade | DISCOs or large customers in India/Bangladesh | A generator deals directly with overseas buyers. The PDF ties this to larger projects, investor participation, and more complex transmission and contracting requirements. | # Commercial modes layered on top of those channels The executive summary then adds a second distinction that should not be collapsed into the buyer categories above: Long-term PPA mode: PPAs with NEA, with a trading company, or directly with foreign distribution companies / major customers. Market-linked mode: power sold through trading-company arrangements into exchange-style markets such as IEX, including the green-market discussion in the surrounding text. That is why the section reads as more than a simple "three-scheme" menu. The PDF clearly names three buyer categories, but it also makes room for multiple price-setting and dispatch arrangements inside them. # Why this matters to the wiki It clarifies that Nepal is not moving from one monolithic single-buyer system to one monolithic export system. The IPSDP is imagining a mixed commercial stack. It sharpens india-energy-relationship and bangladesh-trade-route by showing that cross-border trade is not just a transmission problem; it is also a buyer-structure and contract-structure problem. It explains why niettp and other interconnection corridors are enabling infrastructure rather than the whole trade model by themselves. # Confidence boundary This page sticks to what the executive summary says explicitly on pages 24-26. It does not claim that Nepal has already implemented this market structure. The document is a planning frame, not proof of execution. # Related india-energy-relationship bangladesh-trade-route niettp seasonal-arbitrage-trap

pages.title: Peak Water
headings: Mechanism: more melt before less water
Why it matters for hydropower planning
The Karnali decomposition: what "glacier-fed" actually means
pages.excerpt: Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade
pages.body_text: Peak Water Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade phase where retreat temporarily increases exposed ice area and melt efficiency, then yields to shrinking ice volume and lower dry-season baseflows. # Mechanism: more melt before less water As glaciers retreat, many basins first see higher annual melt — more energy at the surface, longer melt seasons, reconfiguration of flow paths — before entering a regime where ice storage is depleted and late-century flows fall, especially in the low-flow months that hydropower and irrigation care about most. For most Nepal river basins, literature and regional assessments commonly place peak water between ~2040 and 2070, with uncertainty driven by emissions pathway, debris cover, and sub-grid hypsometry. # Why it matters for hydropower planning Nepal's dominant fleet is sensitive to intra-annual and inter-annual hydrographs; see seasonal-mismatch. Peak water defines a planning window: roughly 20-40 years during which glacial contribution is near its maximum for many catchments — not a stable eternity, but a phase where high-head, glacier-fed rivers can look deceptively "strong" in mean annual flow statistics. The climate-rational engineering strategy is dual: 1. Build and finance during peak water where projects clear benefit, consent, and hazard screens — because energy and water yields for glacier-dependent schemes are comparatively favorable. 2. Design for post-peak decline — reservoir operating rules, sediment management, environmental flows, and downstream expectations must assume a worsening dry-season regime, not a stationary 20th-century hydrograph. Basin-specific discharge framing lives in koshi-basin and karnali-basin pages; cryosphere evidence synthesis is anchored on icimod-hkh-glacier-change. # Hazards ride the same trend Retreat does not only alter means; it reconfigures risk. Lake formation, moraine instability, and cascading processes connect peak-water-era construction to glof-risk: the same decades that maximize certain yields also maximize some tail-risk exposures unless design assumptions are updated. Glacial lake area has been expanding at ~0.83%/year since 1987 — a slow accumulation of potential energy with episodic catastrophic release. # Orders of magnitude for urgency Quantitative markers from recent Himalayan climate reviews and ICIMOD: Indicator | Value | Confidence | -----------|-------|------------| Nepal temperature rise rate | 0.02–0.16 °C/year (recent decades) | Moderate — regional variation wide | HKH glacier mass balance | −0.3 to −0.8 m water-equivalent/year | Moderate — peer-reviewed | Glacial lake area expansion | ~0.83%/year since 1987 | Moderate — satellite-derived | Nepal glacier area (1980s -> 2010) | 5,168.3 -> 3,902.4 km² (−24.5%) | Moderate — inventory comparison | Snow/ice cover decline (Nepal) | ~40% documented | Medium — boundary definition varies | Long-run volume loss (high emissions) | ~65% by 2100 across HKH | Low precision — model range is wide | HKH glacier area loss 1990-2020 | ~12% | Moderate — ICIMOD aggregates | The policy point is not precision to the third decimal: it is that Nepal's hydropower golden window is time-bounded and should be used to build durable domestic value, not only nameplate MW. # The Karnali decomposition: what "glacier-fed" actually means A peer-reviewed high-resolution hydrological decomposition of the Karnali basin breaks long-term discharge into contributions: Rainfall runoff: ~40% Baseflow (groundwater): ~35% Snowmelt: ~24% (peaks pre-monsoon, buffers late dry season) Glacier ice melt: ~0.8% of annual volume This is the corrective to the "glacier-fed = reliable" assumption. Glacier ice melt is a tiny share of annual Karnali flow — snowmelt and baseflow are the real dry-season buffers. As warming shifts more precipitation from snow to rain at high elevations and depletes snowpack, the dry-season buffer degrades even before glacier volume is substantially reduced. The near-term risk is loss of snowmelt buffer, not yet major glacier volume loss — the timing of this risk is earlier than most public narratives acknowledge. # Climate change and seasonal amplification Part 4 (DeepResearch) synthesizes the directional consensus: Monsoon precipitation: likely to intensify (heavier events, more flood risk), while total annual rainfall shows mixed regional trends Snowpack: declining as warming shifts precipitation from snow to rain at progressively higher elevations Net seasonal effect: wetter wet seasons, drier dry seasons — amplifying seasonal-mismatch over time Hydropower implication: more extreme flood risk in monsoon (structural and safety design), more acute lean-season deficit (reliability design) — both costs rise simultaneously The seasonal-mismatch that already defines Nepal's energy problem today will structurally worsen over the planning horizon. Storage becomes more valuable, not less, as climate instability increases.

Status legend: done · in progress · open · deferred. # Priority 1–3 · Content depth ## Step 1. Narrate top 10 anchor projects Raise the -tier marker count from 2 to 10+ so the tier filter is useful and the reader has real prose, not just spec tables. [x] Upper Tamakoshi — already narrated (reference template) [x] Budhi Gandaki — narrated, cites WB CEM 2025 [x] Arun-3 — narrated + source page [x] Kali Gandaki A — narrated + source [x] Tanahu — extended + source [x] Dudhkoshi Storage — extended + source [x] Upper Karnali — narrated + source [x] Mugu Karnali — narrated + source [ ] West Seti (Bajhang/Doti, 750 MW, storage; recent Indian re-entry) — reserved for user [ ] Pancheshwar (Darchula/Uttarakhand, 6720 MW, bi-national treaty) — reserved for user Template: bold 1-sentence opener · Specifications table · Significance section · Limitations/Controversies · 2+ primary sources · 3+ inline . ## Step 2.
