Per-slug manifest captures attribution. [x] Batch mode with curated query map for 15 slugs; 50 images pulled on first sweep (Kali Gandaki A, Kulekhani cascade, all four basins all got real in-subject photos; survey-stage projects fell back to district scenery). [x] mode prunes frontmatter entries when images are deleted from disk — user curation workflow confirmed. ## Step 3. Sources & citations discipline [ ] convention on all entity pages [ ] — flags pages with claims but no cites [ ] Require at least one primary source for the top-10 narrated # Priority 4–6 · Graph structure ## Step 4. Backlinks & related pages [x] — scans refs, writes (570+ refs, 71 targets) [x] wiki-loader renders grouped "Referenced by" footer with context snippets [x] Chained into regeneration pipeline [x] Code-span-aware scanner (skips ` foo bindings.json transmission_corridors transmission_traced claim-transmission-immediate-blocker arun-3 khimti-i bangladesh-trade-route narrative_slug presets.json make regenerate Makefile stubs indexes validate all wiki/explorer/shared/build-id.txt validate_wiki.py layers_on features[].layer wikilink .md make validate { preset, layers[], slug, tier, basemap } @media (max-width: 900px) hydropower_operating/construction ≥ 20 MW`) Three-tier marker system + segmented filter Image filmstrip + lightbox from frontmatter Cache-busting for JSON indexes and markdown fetches Curated-layer exemption from tier filter Tier-state banner Popup handshake fixes ("Showing in reader", optimistic currentSlug) "buildReverseIndex" exact-match binding fix # See also hydropower-potential-categories run-of-river-hydropower firm-power

pages.title: Run-of-River Hydropower
pages.excerpt: Nepal’s power system is dominated by run-of-river (RoR) hydropower: plants that divert part of a river through headworks and a tunnel, then return it downstream without holding a seasonal inventory of water. RoR is not “small hydro” and not automatically low-impact—it is an archi
pages.body_text: Run-of-River Hydropower Nepal’s power system is dominated by run-of-river (RoR) hydropower: plants that divert part of a river through headworks and a tunnel, then return it downstream without holding a seasonal inventory of water. RoR is not “small hydro” and not automatically low-impact—it is an architecture defined by what you do not store. # RoR vs PRoR vs storage Architecture | Water use | Seasonal carryover | Typical role | ----------------|-----------|--------------------|----------------| RoR | Uses contemporaneous river flow | None | Energy follows the hydrograph | Peaking RoR (PRoR) | Short pondage (hours) for shifting output within a day | Not seasonal storage; usually 2–6 hours of useful peaking | Better intraday shape, still hostage to the monsoon | Reservoir / storage | Large impoundment | Yes—wet-season inflow can be banked for dry-season release | Seasonal firming and strategic reserves | Fleet composition matters analytically: Nepal’s installed hydro is roughly ~85.7% RoR, ~9.9% PRoR, and ~3.7% storage (see data-fleet-composition). That single sentence is already a forecast: most megawatts are structurally aligned with wet-season inflow, not with steady demand. # Why Nepal favored RoR The political economy pushed toward RoR for coherent reasons: lower unit cost than large storage, faster financial close for private IPPs, less resettlement than big reservoirs, and a regulatory/licensing path that matched export-oriented and domestic “capacity addition” narratives. RoR also fragments risk across many sponsors instead of concentrating it in one megaproject state balance sheet. The trade-off is strategic: RoR solved how to add MW quickly without solving how to add seasonal value. More RoR in a monsoon climate does not average out into “more reliable power”—it tends to deepen the same dual outcome: surplus when the river is high and deficit when it is low (see seasonal-mismatch). # Fleet type as destiny In the medium term, fleet type is destiny: you cannot wish PRoR pondage into seasonal storage, and you cannot dispatch what physics never impounded. The rare counterexamples—upper-tamakoshi as a comparatively “wet-season heavy” large RoR story, and kulekhani-cascade as almost the entire operational storage base—illustrate how narrow the exceptions are. For planning, the operative question is not headline megawatts but what kind of electricity those megawatts become across months: that is the bridge from RoR economics to firm-power and the physical gap summarized as storage-deficit.

pages.title: Seasonal Arbitrage Trap
headings: The numbers that look like success
The monthly table: the trap made visible at full resolution
Why this is structural, not accidental
Two price realities: IEX vs whole-system
pages.excerpt: The seasonal arbitrage trap describes how Nepal can appear to "win" on electricity trade while losing on economic value: the country systematically sells cheap wet-season surplus and buys expensive dry-season power, so headline trade balances mask a destroyed margin on timing.
pages.body_text: Seasonal Arbitrage Trap The seasonal arbitrage trap describes how Nepal can appear to "win" on electricity trade while losing on economic value: the country systematically sells cheap wet-season surplus and buys expensive dry-season power, so headline trade balances mask a destroyed margin on timing. # The numbers that look like success Reported averages illustrate the wedge: exports have averaged roughly NPR 7.11–8.72 per unit (depending on year and channel), while imports cluster around NPR 9.17–16 per unit—a large spread that reflects when each transaction occurs, not inefficiency in a single market. In FY 2023/24, Nepal exported about NPR 17.1 billion and imported about NPR 16.9 billion, leaving a net of only ~NPR 200 million despite being described as a "net exporter." In FY 2024/25, the headline improved — exports NPR 17.46B vs imports NPR 12.92B, net surplus ~NPR 4.5B. But the monthly pattern exposes the trap's persistence: first-half FY 2025/26 data shows peak monthly revenue of NPR 5.03B (Ashoj/Sep–Oct) collapsing to NPR 1.10B (Mangsir/Nov–Dec) before exports stop entirely and imports begin — a 4.6x swing within a single fiscal year (see data-trade-time-series). # The monthly table: the trap made visible at full resolution The NEA System Operation Department's FY 2079/80 (~2022/23) operational report provides month-by-month GWh data — the first full monthly resolution in the entire research corpus. Nepal's four biggest import months (Magh, Falgun, Chaitra, Baisakh) record zero exports. Baisakh alone logged 395.7 GWh imported, zero exported. A single peak winter month absorbs more import cost than any single monsoon month earns in export revenue. A concrete dry-season snapshot: Falgun 2080 (mid-Feb to mid-Mar 2024) saw 406.3 GWh imported in one month, peak import hitting 643 MW on a single evening around 18:30–18:55 — the daily cooking-and-lighting peak — when run-of-river output was at its annual floor. Even with 3,591 MW installed, Nepal approved imports of up to 654 MW for Jan–Mar 2026 to cover winter peak demand. See data-trade-time-series for the full month-by-month breakdown. # Why this is structural, not accidental The pattern is the trade manifestation of seasonal-mismatch: a run-of-river–heavy fleet produces when rivers are high and must import when rivers are low. Without large-scale seasonal shifting — what storage-deficit summarizes as Nepal's physical gap — arbitrage runs against the domestic system rather than for it. India's grid and approval corridors matter because they define when cross-border capacity is usable and at what effective price; see india-energy-relationship. # Two price realities: IEX vs whole-system The trap operates on two layers that must not be conflated: Exchange channel (IEX): Nepal's weighted-average IEX sell price exceeded its buy price in 2023-24 — ₹5.61/kWh sell vs ₹4.43/kWh buy, a +₹1.18/kWh positive spread. In 2022-23 the spread was negative (buy ₹5.95, sell ₹5.14 — a −₹0.81 penalty). The improvement is real: Nepal is competing more effectively in India's real-time market during high-hydro months. Whole-system average: In the same FY 2023/24, Nepal's overall sale price averaged Rs 8.72/unit while purchase averaged Rs 9.17/unit — a negative spread of Rs 0.45/unit across the full portfolio. The gap between the positive IEX spread and the negative system average is explained by structural winter imports at constrained, high-cost rates (peak hours, non-exchange procurement) that wash out the monsoon exchange gains. Even in its best recent trade year, Nepal was a net payer per kWh when all transactions are averaged. # Policy signals: pricing dry-season scarcity Power purchase and tariff design are beginning to encode scarcity by season. PPA restructuring for storage projects splits winter (~NPR 14.80/unit) from monsoon (~NPR 8.45/unit) — an admission that dry-season energy is a different commodity. Nepal's industrial time-of-day tariffs confirm the same logic: peak hours (17:00–23:00) at ~NPR 10.00–10.20/kWh vs off-peak at ~NPR 4.65–5.25/kWh. The system prices scarcity; it just cannot yet produce the peak supply from its own fleet. # Strategic implication Nepal is currently resource-rich but value-poor because timing destroys the margin. The cleanest formulation (DeepResearch Part 7): "The system is getting better at monetizing surplus, but it still pays a winter scarcity tax, and it has not yet built enough domestic electrification depth to turn hydropower into broad-based wealth." A domestic-led-hydro-strategy reframes the objective: build and price for domestic dry-season reliability first, then use exports to monetize true surplus — rather than exporting because the alternative is curtailment at bad prices. Empirical trade dynamics belong in data-trade-time-series.

pages.title: Seasonal Mismatch
headings: The hydrograph vs the load duration curve
Fixes are physical, financial, or institutional—often all three
pages.excerpt: Seasonal mismatch is the central structural problem of Nepal’s hydropower-heavy system: inflow and demand follow different calendars. It is not a one-off drought story and not primarily a “we need more MW” story—it is what happens when a monsoon river regime meets a comparatively
pages.body_text: Seasonal Mismatch Seasonal mismatch is the central structural problem of Nepal’s hydropower-heavy system: inflow and demand follow different calendars. It is not a one-off drought story and not primarily a “we need more MW” story—it is what happens when a monsoon river regime meets a comparatively year-round electricity demand profile under a run-of-river–dominated fleet (see run-of-river-hydropower). # The hydrograph vs the load duration curve In Nepal’s major rivers, a large share of annual discharge arrives in the wet season (commonly cited as 75%+ between June and September in basin planning discourse). RoR plants therefore produce a wet-season surplus and a dry-season deficit simultaneously—two faces of the same physics. Operational anecdotes and system reporting cohere around a brutal swing: system-relevant generation often peaks in August–September in the ~2,200–2,500 MW range, then contracts in January–February toward roughly 600–900 MW—on the order of a 60–65% decline from the monsoon crest. Demand, by contrast, is comparatively smooth: winter heating and structural load growth do not “turn off” when the rivers fall. The result is a recurring annual pattern: large monsoon surplus paired with material winter deficit. This is a structural property of RoR hydro in a monsoon climate, not a temporary market glitch. # Why “more RoR” amplifies both sides Adding RoR capacity is not neutral: it increases energy when the system is already long, and it does little to change the dry-season floor unless accompanied by storage, diversified winter resources, or imports/exports that are actually available when domestic rivers are low. This is why debates about “potential MW” often miss the point captured in claim-timing-not-volume: timing can dominate volume in value. # Fixes are physical, financial, or institutional—often all three Only a small set of interventions change the mismatch itself rather than reshuffling who suffers it: Storage that shifts water across seasons (Nepal’s gap is stark: storage-deficit) Alternative winter sources (imports, gas, solar+storage at scale, industrial demand response—each with constraints) Market and dispatch design that prices scarcity and curtailment honestly—without pretending PRoR pondage is seasonal banking (see seasonal-arbitrage-trap) Basin-scale discharge framing and indicators belong in data-basin-discharge. The analytical lens that separates “installed capacity” from what the system can depend on is firm-power.

pages.title: Sediment as Design Constraint
headings: Orders-of-magnitude swings are normal, not exceptional
Kali Gandaki A: the field test that tells the real story
Francis clearance gaps as a parable of wrong-kind-of-precision
The Khimti benchmark: what best practice looks like
pages.excerpt: In Nepal, sediment is often discussed as an operations headache — rakers, flushes, lost efficiency. That understates the issue. Sediment is a core design constraint: it determines what kind of plant you can build, what turbine you can credibly run, how fast maintenance capital is
pages.body_text: Sediment as Design Constraint In Nepal, sediment is often discussed as an operations headache — rakers, flushes, lost efficiency. That understates the issue. Sediment is a core design constraint: it determines what kind of plant you can build, what turbine you can credibly run, how fast maintenance capital is consumed, and whether a reservoir's economic life matches its financial model. # Orders-of-magnitude swings are normal, not exceptional Case evidence from Himalayan plants shows concentration swings that are almost incomprehensible outside high mountain rivers. At Kali Gandaki A style plants: dry-season suspended sediment on the order of ~20 ppm climbs to monsoon peaks on the order of ~50,000 ppm — not a gentle seasonal tilt but a 2,500x regime change in fluid abrasiveness. At that scale, tens of millions of tonnes per year pass the dam axis while millions of tonnes per year move through turbines — turning "a little silt" into a metal removal process measured in millimeters per season. # Kali Gandaki A: the field test that tells the real story A thermodynamic field test of three Francis units at Kali Gandaki A (Nepal's largest NEA-owned RoR plant) conducted in accordance with IEC 60041 provides the only direct Nepal plant-efficiency measurement in the entire research corpus: Best-point efficiency: ~90–92% depending on unit condition and time since overhaul The oldest unit (two years since last overhaul) had materially lower best-point efficiency than the recently refurbished unit The study explicitly traces the degradation to erosion of guide vane and facing plate geometry and leakage growth through labyrinth seals — i.e., abrasion physically converts a precision machine into an increasingly leaky one between overhauls Hard coatings including HVOF tungsten carbide are being applied, but wear on runners, guide vanes, facing plates, and labyrinth rings reoccurs after each monsoon This is "efficiency left on the table" in the most literal sense: the gap between a freshly refurbished unit and a two-year-worn unit, multiplied across a fleet of dozens of plants, represents recoverable generation that does not require new MW — just sediment discipline. # Francis clearance gaps as a parable of wrong-kind-of-precision A commonly documented maintenance case: a Francis runner designed with ~0.6 mm clearance erodes to ~1.35 mm in one monsoon year — more than doubling, with cascading effects on efficiency, cavitation margins, and vibration signatures. Mitigations — HVOF coatings, NDT campaigns, shortened overhaul intervals — are not "nice to have"; they are capitalized lifecycle costs that belong in LCOE and investor due diligence from day one. For high-head impulse machines: a Pelton installation (22 MW) case study documents sand erosion of nozzles and buckets after a single monsoon season — consistent with what is seen globally in sediment-laden high-head systems. # The Khimti benchmark: what best practice looks like The 60 MW Khimti RoR plant in Dolakha is documented (by a sediment engineering practitioner) as having exceptionally comprehensive sediment removal for a run-of-river plant: Stone trap → Gravel trap → Large desanders (multi-chamber) → Additional tunnel sediment traps Sized beyond standard 0.2 mm criteria to remove nearly all particles above ~0.13 mm The multi-stage cascade approach — coarse removal first, then progressively finer — is the engineering standard Nepal should be applying consistently but is not Khimti demonstrates Nepal is capable of best practice. The problem is that best practice is not consistently embedded across the buildout, especially when intake land constraints and cost pressure force compromises. # Forced outages: sediment's operational footprint A Nepal engineering study analyzing five years of operational data for four utility-owned PRoR plants identifies the distribution of forced outages by cause. 80% of forced outages were driven by turbine, generator, and excitation system failures — exactly the components most exposed to sediment abrasion. Availability figures look high on paper (~99% range), but the recurring failure modes point to a fleet operating under chronic stress from its operating environment. # Design and technology selection implications Sediment pushes engineering toward: Robust turbine families — Pelton preference where head allows (no runner blades in the suspended sediment path during normal operation, though nozzle erosion remains) Multi-stage sediment removal at intake (stone trap → gravel trap → desander → tunnel traps) with flushing discipline rather than just settling Sediment-aware operations — threshold-based shutdown during extreme sediment events (Bhutan's approach: switch-off intake above a turbidity threshold to protect runners) Real-time sediment monitoring instruments as operational infrastructure, not just research tools — enables decisions rather than only post-damage audits Shorter payback horizons and conservative debt sizing — because the river, not the spreadsheet, sets wear rates Reservoir sediment bypass tunnels (Japan and Switzerland model) for future storage projects — routing sediment-laden flood flows around the reservoir and turbines during peak monsoon Basin context matters: sediment regimes differ systematically across gandaki-basin and koshi-basin corridors; climate-driven landslide connectivity can tighten constraints over time. Buildability (buildability) is partly "can you keep the runner in spec?" Sediment-linked hazards also overlap with glof-risk as part of the same mountain hazard stack. # The technology roadmap implication Nepal should treat abrasion control as an efficiency program, not merely a maintenance program. The field data show measurable efficiency degradation over time and link it to leakage and erosion — meaning abrasion control is "recoverable generation" expressed in NPR/year per plant, not just O&M cost lines. At scale across 189 plants, systematic sediment-fit upgrading could recover generation that exceeds what several new RoR plants would add.

pages.title: Storage Deficit
headings: Why storage is politically hard even when physically “obvious”
pages.excerpt: Nepal’s energy crisis is often narrated as a shortage of projects. At the water–energy interface, the sharper truth is often a shortage of seasonal inventory: storage deficit—the gap between the impoundment capacity needed to re-time monsoon water to dry-season scarcity and what
pages.body_text: Storage Deficit Nepal’s energy crisis is often narrated as a shortage of projects. At the water–energy interface, the sharper truth is often a shortage of seasonal inventory: storage deficit—the gap between the impoundment capacity needed to re-time monsoon water to dry-season scarcity and what actually exists on the ground. # Orders of magnitude International comparisons are sobering: Nepal is frequently placed around ~5 m³ per person of storage, versus rough reference magnitudes such as India ~180, China ~664, Norway ~15,100 (see data-storage-comparison for the project table and caveats). Nepal’s operational hydropower storage is essentially kulekhani-cascade—on the order of 106 MW with storage volumes commonly cited around 85–150 MCM—a critical asset that is nonetheless tiny relative to national scale. World Bank water diagnostics have framed multi-decadal storage needs at extraordinary volumes—on the order of 29.86 km³ in one national diagnostic framing—while noting that effective seasonal storage for that purpose is effectively absent. Whether one takes that headline literally or as an order-of-magnitude provocation, the ratio implies something like a ~200× mismatch between aspirational storage need and operational reality: the exact factor matters less than the sign—Nepal is storage-poor in both water security and power-firming terms. # Topography as paradox Nepal’s steep gradients are good for head and therefore for RoR hydropower, but narrow V-shaped valleys work against large gentle reservoirs at acceptable inundation. The contrast with classic big-basin storage (e.g., Aswan-scale volumes from modest dam heights) is instructive: Nepal can get “big numbers” on paper from river energy, yet struggle to find low-loss, low-conflict reservoir geometries—see discussions around budhigandaki and dudhkoshi-storage. # Why storage is politically hard even when physically “obvious” Seasonal mismatch (seasonal-mismatch) makes storage the clearest physical answer, but projects collide with sedimentation, seismic hazard, resettlement, financing, and treaty-dependent downstream politics. That is why buildability matters as much as MW: a reservoir is not a line on a map until the whole engineering–social–finance package survives scrutiny.

pages.title: Stranded Generation
headings: Monsoon surpluses meet hard export and internal bottlenecks
pages.excerpt: Stranded generation is the condition where plants could produce but the grid cannot absorb or move the energy—so operators spill water, throttle output, or receive dispatch orders to stop. In Nepal, this is increasingly a monsoon phenomenon tied to a simple mismatch: generation b
pages.body_text: Stranded Generation Stranded generation is the condition where plants could produce but the grid cannot absorb or move the energy—so operators spill water, throttle output, or receive dispatch orders to stop. In Nepal, this is increasingly a monsoon phenomenon tied to a simple mismatch: generation buildout has raced ahead of transmission and market access. # Monsoon surpluses meet hard export and internal bottlenecks During wet-season peaks, domestic demand is insufficient to swallow RoR-heavy output (see seasonal-mismatch). Export is the obvious relief valve—but export is not “infinite outlet”: it is constrained by cross-border approval, contracted corridors, and internal backbone capacity. Illustrative orders of magnitude from recent public reporting cycles include ~1,400 MW of surplus conditions against only ~1,141 MW approved for export, with internal constraints still producing ~700–800 MW of daily spill/curtailment in severe weeks—even affecting nea’s own plants. Those numbers drift year to year; the structural point is stable: without absorption, more RoR does not become more “used” electricity. # Small IPPs at the knife-edge Curtailment is socially and financially asymmetric. Large plants negotiate visibility; small IPPs (<10 MW) can be ordered to halt by the Load Dispatch Center when operators judge stability margins are threatened. For a leveraged rural RoR project, zero-generation weeks are not a cash-flow smoothing problem—they are covenant-breaking events. That dynamic links stranded energy to buildability in the financial sense: a “cheap” project that cannot run in the season it was built for is expensive. # Transmission as national economic infrastructure Geography concentrates stress: the corridor west of Butwal has relied heavily on legacy 132 kV lines in narratives of congestion, while multilateral packages (for example ADB-backed reinforcement on the order of ~$311M) illustrate the capital scale of partial fixes. Export-ambition scenarios citing ~10,000 MW of export capacity imply transmission and converter investment on the order of ~$5–8B—numbers that land around ~12–19% of GDP in some macro framings—i.e., not a sector line item but a state-building commitment. # Analytical links Stranded energy is the physical mirror of seasonal-arbitrage-trap: surplus energy without priced scarcity, dispatchable demand, or firm export paths does not become welfare—it becomes spill. The institutional counterpart is whether nea and regulators can align incentives so that “installed MW” stops diverging from delivered MWh.

pages.title: Bottleneck Hierarchy
pages.excerpt: Ranked bottlenecks for Nepal's hydropower system, synthesized across all providers. The ranking carries an important nuance: the answer changes depending on whether you ask about deep structural cause or immediate practical blocker.
pages.body_text: Bottleneck Hierarchy Ranked bottlenecks for Nepal's hydropower system, synthesized across all providers. The ranking carries an important nuance: the answer changes depending on whether you ask about deep structural cause or immediate practical blocker. # Tier 1 — Binding Constraints Rank | Bottleneck | Nature | Claim | ------|-----------|--------|-------| 1 | storage-deficit / seasonal-mismatch | Deep structural | claim-storage-physical-fix | 2 | Transmission + delivery readiness | Immediate practical | claim-transmission-immediate-blocker | 3 | Execution / buildability | Persistent | — | # Tier 2 — Shaping Constraints Rank | Bottleneck | Nature | ------|-----------|--------| 4 | Market design / offtake / demand weakness | seasonal-arbitrage-trap | 5 | Climate / hazard / sediment non-stationarity | claim-climate-harder-not-easier | 6 | Project finance / institutional coordination | claim-governance-binding | # Tier 3 — Downstream Constraints Rank | Bottleneck | Nature | ------|-----------|--------| 7 | Geopolitical leverage capture | hydro-geopolitics | # The Critical Nuance If the question is "What is the deep structural cause?" → Storage. The country cannot hold its water. If the question is "Why doesn't more MW quickly convert into value right now?" → Transmission and delivery readiness. Generation is racing ahead of grid. If the question is "Why can't Nepal fix either one?" → Governance and buildability. This is not a contradiction. It is a more serious systems view. # Provider Divergence Provider | Single biggest bottleneck | Notable difference | ----------|-------------------------|-------------------| Hermes | Storage (deep) / Transmission (immediate) | Explicit nuance between deep vs immediate | Claude | Transmission & market access | Argues grid is 2024's binding constraint; ~40/35/25% split | Codex | Storage + grid depth together | Less willing to rank one above the other | Gemini | Storage → Grid → Demand creation | Tripartite framing; adds demand creation as third pillar | # Related master-thesis — the overarching argument twenty-year-strategy — how to address bottlenecks in sequence claim-systems-conversion-failure — the integrated failure diagnosis

pages.title: IPSDP 2025 vs Wiki Claims
headings: Where the IPSDP stays more optimistic than the wiki
What the IPSDP does not overturn
pages.excerpt: This page is a narrow comparison between the state-planning frame in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 and the wiki's higher-level strategic claims. It only records points that the executive summary states clearly enough to compare without guesswork.
pages.body_text: IPSDP 2025 vs Wiki Claims This page is a narrow comparison between the state-planning frame in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 and the wiki's higher-level strategic claims. It only records points that the executive summary states clearly enough to compare without guesswork. # What the IPSDP sharpens Against twenty-year-strategy, the IPSDP adds a dated official ladder: export crossover in 2025, self-sufficiency in 2033, the 28 GW government target in 2035, and clean-export contribution in 2040. Against master-thesis, the IPSDP gives the most current state-authored sequencing logic for generation, backbone transmission, interconnection, and financing in one place. Against domestic-led-hydro-strategy, the ladder is notable because self-sufficiency comes before the later regional clean-export role. That sequencing matters. # Where the IPSDP stays more optimistic than the wiki The IPSDP is a planning document. It assumes institutional reform, finance mobilization, transmission buildout, and project execution can be made to align on schedule. The wiki's more skeptical pages such as buildability, stranded-generation, and seasonal-arbitrage-trap focus on the reasons those alignments often fail in practice. So the executive summary should be read as an official target stack, not as proof that the target stack is already deliverable. # What the IPSDP does not overturn It does not overturn master-thesis. The IPSDP still acknowledges transmission, finance, governance, and environmental constraints; it simply organizes them inside a more optimistic execution pathway. It does not invalidate domestic-led-hydro-strategy. The document is still export-aware and cross-border in orientation, but its own milestone ordering does not put regional clean-export contribution ahead of domestic self-sufficiency. It does not resolve the wiki's skepticism about timing-value destruction in seasonal-arbitrage-trap. The trade section expands market channels, but it does not claim that Nepal has already escaped the seasonal structure of cheap monsoon sales and costly dry-season imports. # Practical use Use the IPSDP when you need the strongest current official statement of target timing and system architecture. Use the broader wiki when you need the stress test on whether those targets survive geology, seasonal hydrology, grid delivery, and political execution. # Related moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 master-thesis twenty-year-strategy domestic-led-hydro-strategy

pages.title: Master Thesis
pages.excerpt: The central argument of the Nepal Energy & Hydropower research, supported by all five providers (Hermes, Claude, Codex, Gemini, DeepResearch/ChatGPT).
pages.body_text: Master Thesis The central argument of the Nepal Energy & Hydropower research, supported by all five providers (Hermes, Claude, Codex, Gemini, DeepResearch/ChatGPT). # The Thesis Nepal is not failing because it lacks hydropower potential. It is failing to convert hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, resilient, financeable, politically executable power. See claim-systems-conversion-failure. # The Failure Stack The conversion failure is not one bottleneck. It is a stack: 1. seasonal-mismatch — 75%+ of water in four months; hydrology delivers a monsoon pulse, not year-round firm power 2. run-of-river-hydropower — fleet is >90% RoR/PRoR; structurally produces wet-season surplus and dry-season weakness 3. storage-deficit — 5 m³/capita vs global average 900; the country cannot hold and regulate its water 4. sediment-as-design-constraint and climate penalties — the Himalayas are young, active, and abrasive 5. buildability — brutal terrain, geology, access, and execution constraints 6. stranded-generation — grid and transmission built slower than generation 7. seasonal-arbitrage-trap — selling cheap monsoon, buying expensive winter; value destroyed by timing 8. hydro-geopolitics and governance — corridor dependence on India, institutional fragmentation, political churn # What the Thesis Is Not It is not "Nepal has no hydro potential" — the resource base is real It is not "Nepal should stop building hydro" — the country needs more, but of the right kind It is not "exports are bad" — exports are necessary but should not define the master strategy It is not "India is the enemy" — India is the decisive market interface, and Nepal must work through that structure # The Strategic Prescription domestic-led-hydro-strategy — build for domestic dry-season reliability and productive demand first, use exports to monetize surplus. Grounded in storage, transmission, demand shaping, and institutional repair. Not in headline MW worship. # Supporting Evidence bottleneck-hierarchy — ranked constraints twenty-year-strategy — phased implementation data-potential-pyramid — the 83,000 to 3,591 reduction data-storage-comparison — the storage gap in numbers data-trade-time-series — the seasonal trade pattern # Framing Lines for Video Essay "Nepal is not energy-poor. It is dispatch-poor." — Codex "Water becomes electricity only when it is timed. Electricity becomes wealth only when it is delivered. And hydropower becomes strategy only when it is regulated." — Codex report "Nepal's greatest missed opportunity is not selling electricity to India at USD 0.04/kWh — it is using that electricity to manufacture goods worth USD 0.40/kWh." — Claude "The system is getting better at monetizing surplus, but it still pays a winter scarcity tax, and it has not yet built enough domestic electrification depth to turn hydropower into broad-based wealth." — DeepResearch Part 7 "Nepal is not failing because it lacks hydropower potential. It is failing to convert hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, resilient, financeable, politically executable power." — This project's synthesis

pages.title: Provider Comparison
pages.excerpt: Condensed assessment of the four AI providers' research outputs. Full assessment at in project root.
pages.body_text: Provider Comparison Condensed assessment of the four AI providers' research outputs. Full assessment at in project root. # Summary Table Provider | Approach | Key Strength | Key Weakness | Unique Contribution | ----------|----------|-------------|--------------|---------------------| Hermes | Multi-wave research program | Process discipline, claim tracking | Weaker on hard numbers; no narrative report | Claim ledger, confidence ratings, wave methodology | Claude | Two DOCX deliverables (QA + report) | Best narrative prose; strongest comparisons | No source citations | Bhutan comparison, treaty specifics, industrial value multiplier | Codex | Two markdown deliverables with sourcing | Best-sourced; most current data | More restrained interpretation | FY 2024/25 NEA data, 15 verified URLs, "dispatch-poor" framing | Gemini | Single 22-page PDF report | Deepest engineering + economics detail | Occasional editorial overreach | Kali Gandaki turbine case, green hydrogen, PPA pricing, 74 sources | # Thesis Convergence All four converge on: claim-systems-conversion-failure Sub-theses also converge: Timing > volume (claim-timing-not-volume) Storage is the physical fix (claim-storage-physical-fix) Transmission is the immediate blocker (claim-transmission-immediate-blocker) Domestic use > export worship (claim-domestic-led-strategy) India is decisive (claim-india-decisive-actor) Governance is binding (claim-governance-binding) # Source Quality Provider | Sources cited | Currency | ----------|-------------|----------| Hermes | ~8 primary (extracted to files) | FY 2022/23–2024 | Claude | None cited inline | Up to ~2024 | Codex | 15 numbered with URLs | FY 2024/25 (most current) | Gemini | 74 works cited with URLs | Up to early 2026 | # Recommendation for Master Document Hermes as analytical backbone (claim ledger, confidence tracking) Codex as primary data layer (most current, best-sourced) Claude as narrative and comparative layer (best prose, unique framings) Gemini as specialist depth layer (engineering, pricing, hydrogen, grid spillage) # Related master-thesis — what all providers agree on unresolved-questions — what none of them resolved

pages.title: Twenty-Year Strategy
pages.excerpt: A rational 20-year water-and-energy strategy for Nepal, synthesized from Claude's phased framework and cross-referenced with all providers.
pages.body_text: Twenty-Year Strategy A rational 20-year water-and-energy strategy for Nepal, synthesized from Claude's phased framework and cross-referenced with all providers. # Phase 1: 2024–2030 — Complete and Connect Complete all projects under construction (including tanahu-hydropower) Build Butwal-Gorakhpur cross-border line Begin east-west 400 kV transmission backbone Negotiate Bangladesh electricity transit arrangement through India Pilot first pumped storage (200–500 MW) Use cheap power to begin attracting energy-intensive industry Priority: Fix stranded-generation by building grid before licensing more generation. # Phase 2: 2030–2037 — Store and Industrialize Develop karnali-basin corridor (first 2,000 MW) Complete east-west transmission backbone Add two more India cross-border lines Build budhigandaki (1,200 MW storage) Activate Bangladesh export contracts at scale Use cheap reliable power to attract ferrosilicon, cement, and green-hydrogen-nepal investment Priority: Add storage-deficit relief. Convert from seasonal surplus to year-round system value. # Phase 3: 2037–2044 — Leverage and Transform 10,000+ MW installed Major storage operational Industrial electricity demand absorbing 30–40% of generation Export revenue $500M+/year Renegotiate Koshi and Gandak treaties from position of infrastructure strength Design for peak-water post-peak decline Priority: Use energy as foundation for domestic-led-hydro-strategy economic transformation. # Six Principles 1. Move from capacity maximization to firm-power maximization 2. Build storage deliberately, not symbolically — sequenced around highest system value 3. Treat transmission as part of generation economics — every basin plan judged with evacuation 4. Build domestic demand at the same time — don't wait for industry to appear 5. Upgrade for climate resilience — better hydromet, sediment-as-design-constraint monitoring, glof-risk design standards 6. Think in basins, not just projects — project-by-project opportunism is the wrong scale # Precondition All of this requires political stability enabling multi-decade commitment and nea capability building that matches ambition. Without that, the engineering plans are fiction. # Related bottleneck-hierarchy — what this strategy addresses in sequence master-thesis — why this approach, not "build more hydro"
